In the working-hours guard, end is computed from slotStartTime instead of the slot's end time, so longer meetings can pass even if they extend past workingHour.endTime, making slots appear available outside normal hours

The workingHours.find predicate returns true when a slot is outside a particular workingHour, so if there are multiple working-hour ranges for the same day, a slot valid in one range can be rejected because it's outside another, causing false unavailable results for split schedules
override.timeZone is optional on TimeRange but is assumed present when calling tz(override.timeZone) - if undefined, the offset math may be incorrect or throw depending on Dayjs config

propel.text: checkIfIsAvailable returns true immediately when dateOverrideExist is true, skipping busy event checks, seat availability checks, and subsequent guards, which allows double-booking when a fixed host has a date override but already has an accepted booking at the same time

dayjs comparison using === compares object references instead of values, so zero-duration date overrides are never properly detected and dateOverrideExist incorrectly stays true - should use .isSame() method instead

baz.text: dateOverrides.find compares Dayjs instances with === which always fails by reference because Dayjs instances are objects, so zero-length overrides (start === end) never mark slots as busy - should use .isSame() or .valueOf() to compare timestamps
utcOffset adjustments need to be consistently applied to both start and end before comparing in the dateOverrides.find callback

end is derived from slotStartTime instead of slotEndTime, so multi-minute slots that stretch past workingHour.endTime still pass the guard and can book outside of configured working hours - end should be derived from slotEndTime to include the event length

coderabbit.text: Line 114 compares Dayjs objects with === which always returns false - should use .isSame() for Dayjs comparison

Lines 102-140: .find() returns on first override that doesn't contain the slot, causing false rejection even if another override on the same day does - should use .filter() to collect all overrides for the day, then .some() to check if any contain the slot
Line 147 uses slotStartTime for both start and end instead of slotEndTime, ignoring event length in working hours boundary check

Lines 139-144: End time boundary not checked correctly for working hours - end is computed from slotStartTime instead of slotEndTime, allowing slots that extend past workingHour.endTime to be marked as available
Lines 210-224: Missing guard against undefined override.timeZone which may cause dayjs to default to local timezone and mis-shift overrides - should add fallback to organizerTimeZone

greptile.text: `start` and `end` are set to identical values, meaning the slot end time is never checked against working hours
Dayjs objects compared with `===` will never be equal (compares by reference, not value). Use `.isSame()` instead

kg.text: Using dayjs(override.start.toString()) parses the Date as local time string, which is incorrect if the Date represents UTC time - should use dayjs.utc(override.start) instead
dayjs.tz(date.start, organizerTimeZone) treats date.start as local time, but it should be UTC - should use dayjs.utc(date.start).tz(organizerTimeZone)
Logic error in working hours check: end is set to the same as start, but should be slotEndTime.minute() - this causes the check for slots ending after working hours to fail

qodo-v2.text: Dayjs equality comparison uses === which is reference equality and will always be false, making the intended special-case branch unreachable for date overrides where start and end represent the same instant

Multiple date override ranges on the same day are treated as intersection (slot must be inside all ranges) rather than union (slot valid if inside any range) due to find() logic rejecting slots outside any single override range
Working-hours validation computes both start and end from slotStartTime instead of using slotEndTime for end, allowing slots that extend past workingHour.endTime
Date override offset calculation uses Date.toString() and re-parses it via Dayjs which is redundant and may cause subtle parse/precision issues across runtimes

devin.text: Dayjs object reference comparison with `===` always evaluates to false - at line 114, two independently created dayjs objects are compared using `===` which performs reference equality, so the comparison will always be false even when timestamps are identical. This means zero-length date overrides (days marked as unavailable) won't be detected. Should use `.isSame()` instead.

`end` variable incorrectly uses `slotStartTime` instead of `slotEndTime` - at line 142, the `end` variable is calculated using `slotStartTime` which makes it identical to `start`. This breaks the working hours boundary check, allowing slots that extend past working hours to incorrectly pass validation. Should use `slotEndTime` instead.

greptile-v4.text: `end` variable is a copy-paste of `start`, making the working-hours check incorrect - both `start` and `end` are computed from `slotStartTime`, so they are always equal, causing slots whose end time exceeds working hours to incorrectly pass the check

Dayjs objects compared with `===` instead of `.isSame()` - comparing two distinct dayjs objects with `===` compares object references not time values, so the condition is always false and zero-length 'blocked day' overrides are silently skipped

UTC offset is negated, making the day-matching comparison wrong for non-UTC organizer timezones - multiplying by `-1` inverts the sign, causing the date override to never be found for organizers in positive UTC timezones

`dateOverrides` and `workingHours` not included in `availabilityCheckProps` - when `checkIfIsAvailable` is called with only `...availabilityCheckProps` these fields fall back to defaults (`[]`), silently bypassing the new fixed-host override checks

mra-max.text: Date override comparison uses object identity (Dayjs) instead of numeric timestamp - comparing Dayjs objects with === compares object references, not timestamps, causing date override window logic to fail
DST offset calculation relies on current date instead of the slot date, risking incorrect DST adjustments on days with DST transitions
Date overrides handling in checkIfIsAvailable contains an accidental early return that marks slots as unavailable when a date override matches, causing behavioral regression
Viewer endpoint (viewer slots) exposes schedule data without authentication via publicProcedure, potentially leaking host usernames and availability data
High churn in scheduling-related files increases risk of regressions in availability calculation and booking flows
Missing test case for dateOverride with different organizer vs attendee time zones in getSlots
Overly permissive date override check for non-fixed/round-robin hosts - endTime used for evaluating overlap with working hours is incorrectly derived (end equals start)
Potential crash/undefined behavior from unvalidated timeZone input - dayjs.tz may throw or produce incorrect results with invalid time zones
Missing tests for DST transitions, cross-timezone availability, and round-robin vs fixed-host date overrides edge cases
No unit tests covering dateOverride timezone handling in slots.ts getSlots function
Unnecessary unused variable getSlotsTime is initialized but never updated, only logged in debug statement
Missing CHANGELOG.md entry documenting the bug fix affecting date overrides and timezone handling
Documentation gaps around TZ/dateOverride behavior and security boundaries in README and API docs
TimeRange type augmented with optional timeZone field may cause misalignment if clients expect the old shape

mra-nano.text: Date override boundary check uses strict equality (===) on Dayjs objects which compares object references instead of moment values, causing the intended one-off/full-day override case to be effectively ignored and leading to wrong slot availability calculations
Date override UTC offset calculation can crash when timeZone is undefined because dayjs.tz(...).utcOffset() may throw or yield unexpected results when override.timeZone is undefined or falsy

typeof check against undefined value (typeof availabilityCheckProps.currentSeats !== undefined) always evaluates true because it compares a string to the value undefined, causing the guard to never work as intended

timeZone propagation in overrides has conflicting concerns: flattening timeZone into date overrides increases coupling and risks inconsistencies when timeZone is missing, while the TimeRange type change introducing timeZone?: string may cause type drift if consumers pass raw strings instead of Date objects

Timezone date override tests are fragile and sensitive to environment TZ and UTC handling, risking CI failures, and may pass or fail for wrong reasons due to code-level bugs in override offset calculations and boundary logic

mra-ultra.text: Multi-override logic: `find` returns first truthy value, causing incorrect blocking when slot is outside one override but inside another. If a user has multiple date overrides on the same day, and the slot is outside the first override but inside the second, `find` will return the first override and the slot will be incorrectly marked unavailable. The logic needs to check all overrides for the day and only block if the slot is outside ALL of them.

Working hours check logic is inverted — slots inside working hours are incorrectly blocked. The condition returns true when the slot is outside the working hour window, but if any working hour entry does NOT contain the slot, the slot is marked unavailable even if another working hour entry does contain it. Should use `.every()` instead of `.find()`, or invert the condition.

`checkIfIsAvailable` in the `selectedSlots` path does not receive `dateOverrides` or `workingHours`, meaning for events with reserved/selected slots, the new date-override and working-hours logic is completely bypassed.

Active override filter uses UTC start time but `startOfInviteeDay` is in invitee timezone — cross-midnight overrides may be missed. When the invitee is in a timezone significantly ahead of UTC, the UTC representation of the override start may fall outside the invitee's day window even though the override is valid for that day.

Spreading schedule into checkIfIsAvailable passes fields inconsistently across call sites. The `availabilityCheckProps` object omits `dateOverrides` and `workingHours`, relying on spreads at individual call sites to supply them. The `dateOverrides` type also lacks `timeZone` and `userId` fields that are present on the actual objects being spread, creating a silent type mismatch.

mra-b.text: Inconsistent timezone offset logic between getSlots and checkIfIsAvailable - getSlots computes override offset as inviteeUtcOffset - organizerUtcOffset, while checkIfIsAvailable negates only the organizer offset and ignores invitee timezone, causing incorrect date-override filtering for cross-timezone scenarios

Incorrect typeof checks for availabilityCheckProps.currentSeats - typeof returns a string but code compares to undefined value instead of string literal "undefined", causing the first branch to always run (attempting .map on undefined and throwing) and the second branch to never run

mra-a.text: Duplicate date-override/timezone logic implemented in both getTimeSlots (packages/lib/slots.ts) and checkIfIsAvailable (packages/trpc/server/routers/viewer/slots.ts) - both the shared library and server router implement overlapping logic for date overrides and timezone handling

Date override timezone/day-boundary handling issue - activeOverrides filter uses raw UTC moment of override.start compared to invitee's startOfInviteeDay range, but translation to invitee minutes uses timezone-aware offsets, which may incorrectly exclude overrides that should apply after timezone translation

entelligence.text: The `end` variable (line 139) is incorrectly calculated using `slotStartTime` instead of `slotEndTime`, meaning the event duration is ignored when checking if it fits within working hours

Using `.find()` to return `true` when a slot is 'outside' a segment incorrectly rejects slots for users with multiple segments on the same day (e.g., split shifts). A slot at 10:00 is 'outside' a 13:00-17:00 segment, causing the entire day's availability to be rejected even if it fits in another segment. The logic should instead verify if the slot is fully contained within at least one segment for that day

sourcery.text: Working-hours end time uses the slot start time instead of the slot end - both `start` and `end` derive from `slotStartTime`, so the bounds check only considers the slot's start and ignores its duration. `end` should be computed from `slotEndTime` to ensure the entire slot lies within working hours

The sign and use of utcOffset may be reversed - computing the organizer offset then inverting it before adding subtracts the organizer's offset instead of applying it, which could cause off-by-one-day timezone issues

In `checkIfIsAvailable`, the date override logic `.find` callback mutates `dateOverrideExist`, has code paths that don't return a boolean, and uses `===` on Dayjs instances instead of `isSame`, leading to subtle bugs and unclear control flow

cubic-dev.text: Dayjs objects compared by reference (`===`) — this is always `false`. Use `.isSame()` to compare the underlying time values. Without this fix, date overrides representing "unavailable all day" (start === end) will be silently ignored.

`end` is computed from `slotStartTime` instead of `slotEndTime`, making it identical to `start`. This means the working-hours boundary check never validates the slot's end time — slots that extend past working hours will be treated as available.

`override.timeZone` is optional on the `TimeRange` type. If it's `undefined`, `dayjs(...).tz(undefined)` will produce an incorrect UTC offset. Fall back to `organizerTimeZone` (already available in the enclosing `getSlots` scope) to avoid a silent miscalculation.

mesa.text: Critical bug: `start` and `end` are both calculated using the same value (`slotStartTime`). The `end` calculation should use `slotEndTime` instead, causing the working hours check to compare the slot start time against both the startTime and endTime boundaries, which will incorrectly reject valid slots.

Condition uses strict equality (`===`) to compare two Dayjs objects, which will always return `false` since they are different object instances. Should use `.isSame()` instead. This bug could allow bookings when the host marked themselves as unavailable all day.

The tRPC router layer is becoming an overgrown orchestrator managing multiple scheduling concerns (workingHours, dateOverrides, organizerTimeZone). Consider extracting a ScheduleContext object to group related concerns.

Timezone offset calculations are duplicated across the router layer (checkIfIsAvailable) and slots layer (getSlots), creating a maintenance liability where logic drift could occur silently. Should consolidate into a shared utility function.

Override and timezone semantics are scattered across multiple pieces of context (organizerTimeZone, invitee timezone, UTC offsets). The complex temporal logic lacks encapsulation—should extract a dedicated helper like resolveActiveOverridesForDay() to centralize this complexity.
Integration test coverage exists but lacks focused unit tests for DST boundaries and organizer-vs-invitee day crossovers, leaving edge cases unvalidated.

codeant.text: Incorrect Date equality check: comparing dayjs objects with === compares object references, not timestamps, which will always be false. Should use .isSame() instead
Potential incorrect utcOffset sign: multiplying organizer utcOffset by -1 is unclear and may be inconsistent with offset calculations in packages/lib/slots.ts
Slot vs end calculation bug in workingHours check: both start and end variables are set to the same value (slotStartTime) instead of computing end as slotStart + eventLength
Date-override containment logic is complex with inverted logic that may have off-by-one or inclusive/exclusive issues
Inconsistent timezone math paths between packages/lib/slots.ts and checkIfIsAvailable - different offset computation methods may cause bugs
Date override search by day using formatted strings (YYYY MM DD) is fragile - should use dayjs.isSame() with proper timezone conversion instead
Missing unit tests for the helper that converts overrides between timezones
Missing tests for checkIfIsAvailable behavior for slots crossing midnight or DST boundaries
Missing tests covering the workingHours check bug to prevent regression

kodus.text: Incorrect and inconsistent timezone conversion logic for date overrides across multiple files - manual offset calculations or complex logic can lead to incorrect availability calculations

Incorrect calculation for slot end time within working hours check - the end variable uses slotStartTime instead of slotEndTime, causing slots that extend beyond working hours to be incorrectly allowed

In slots.ts lines 141-143, the end variable is assigned using slotStartTime instead of slotEndTime, making it identical to start, so the end boundary check never correctly detects slots extending beyond working hours

Object reference comparison with === between two dayjs instances always returns false because each dayjs() call creates a new object - day-off date overrides where start equals end are never recognized

Logic inversion in working-hours check using Array.find instead of Array.some - when a user has multiple working-hour blocks per day, a slot within the second block is incorrectly marked unavailable because it falls outside the first block

Timezone offset calculation uses opposite sign conventions in the two files - slots.ts uses inviteeUtcOffset - organizerUtcOffset while slots.ts in trpc uses organizerUtcOffset * -1, causing inconsistent slot filtering results

claude-code.text: Line 114 uses === to compare two dayjs objects, which is reference equality and will always be false -- zero-length overrides (blocked days) are never detected. Use .isSame() instead.

Line 123 uses strict isAfter for the end boundary but the start boundary (lines 117-120) uses isBefore || isSame; a slot starting exactly at the override end time passes the check and is incorrectly marked available. Add || slotStartTime.isSame(...) to line 123 for consistency.

Line 142 computes end from slotStartTime instead of slotEndTime, making start and end identical. This means the working hours boundary check end > workingHour.endTime only validates the slot start, not the slot end — so a slot starting within working hours but extending past them will incorrectly be allowed.

When a slot falls within a date override window, checkIfIsAvailable returns true at line 134 without checking the busy array, allowing double bookings during date override periods. The fix: remove the early return true at line 134 and let execution fall through to the busy.every() check at line 153.

kodus-v2.text: Reference equality (===) on two distinct dayjs objects always evaluates to false - dayjs creates separate objects, so the condition to detect full-day-off date overrides never triggers, allowing bookings on days the organizer marked unavailable

Copy-paste error: 'end' is computed from slotStartTime instead of slotEndTime, making 'end' always equal to 'start' - the working-hours boundary check only validates the slot's start minute, never its end, allowing bookings that overflow past working hours

Array.find() short-circuits on the first match - when multiple date overrides exist on the same day, a slot covered by a later override is incorrectly rejected because it falls outside the first override found

qodo-v2-2.text: checkIfIsAvailable computes end from the slot start ignoring event length and uses find() to reject a slot if it is outside any single working-hours range, which breaks users with multiple working-hour windows and can allow slots on days with no working-hours entry

Date override check rejects a slot as soon as it finds any override on that day that the slot is outside of, which breaks days with multiple override ranges since a slot inside a later range can be rejected by an earlier non-matching range

When any date override exists for the slot's day, checkIfIsAvailable can return true before evaluating busy times, which can allow booking a host during calendar-busy time as long as the slot is within the override window

qodo-v22.text: checkIfIsAvailable() returns true when it detects any date override on the slot's day, which bypasses the busy-time overlap logic and can mark conflicting slots as available, allowing double-booking on days with date overrides

Working-hours validation computes end from slotStartTime (ignoring event length) and rejects a slot if it's outside any working-hours segment, even if it's inside another segment on the same day, incorrectly removing valid slots and/or allowing slots that exceed the end boundary
Date override check rejects a slot when it's outside any one override range on that day, which fails when multiple override ranges exist for the same day
Date override logic attempts to compare Dayjs objects with === which is always false and makes that branch dead code

getSlots() computes timezone offsets via dayjs(override.start.toString()).tz(override.timeZone) but timeZone on TimeRange is optional and Date.toString() parsing is locale-dependent, which can produce incorrect offsets or fail when override.timeZone is undefined

qodo-extended.text: checkIfIsAvailable uses === to compare two newly-created Dayjs instances, so an override where start==end is never treated as an all-day override and can incorrectly allow slots that should be blocked
checkIfIsAvailable calculates both start and end from slotStartTime, so a slot that starts within working hours but ends after workingHour.endTime will be incorrectly marked available

When selectedSlots exist, getSchedule calls checkIfIsAvailable without passing workingHours or dateOverrides, so returned availability can ignore normal working hours and date overrides in that code path

getSlots computes timezone offsets by parsing override.start.toString(), which depends on the server's local timezone representation and can yield incorrect offsets and thus wrong slot times for date overrides
When dateOverrideExist is set to true, the function immediately returns true, skipping the busy times check at the end of the function, causing calendar conflicts to be ignored during date overrides

The logic uses find to check if a slot is outside a date override or working hour block. If a user has multiple overrides or working hour blocks on the same day, the slot might be incorrectly marked as unavailable because it falls outside the first evaluated block, even if it fits in another

cubic-v2.text: Dayjs object reference comparison (`===`) is always `false`. Use `.isSame()` to compare values. Because this condition can never be true, a zero-duration date override (start == end, meaning 'unavailable all day') will not be recognized, potentially allowing bookings during a blocked day.

`end` is calculated from `slotStartTime` instead of `slotEndTime`, making it identical to `start`. This means a slot that starts within working hours but extends past the end of working hours will not be filtered out, allowing bookings outside working hours.

qodo-extended-summary.text: checkIfIsAvailable returns true immediately when a date override exists for the slot's day, skipping all busy-time overlap checks and allowing slots during actual conflicts, resulting in double-bookable slots

Working-hours bounds check computes both start and end from slotStartTime, never validating the slot end time against workingHour.endTime, incorrectly allowing slots that run past the end of working hours
getSlots calls .tz(override.timeZone) without guarding, but upstream producers of date overrides commonly provide only {start,end} without timeZone, causing potential runtime exceptions

Two dayjs objects are compared using === (strict reference equality) which always returns false since each dayjs() call creates a new object, breaking detection of zero-length date overrides used to mark days as unavailable

dateOverrides.find() returns truthy when it finds ANY override that the slot doesn't fit within, rather than checking if the slot is outside ALL overrides, incorrectly rejecting valid slots when multiple date overrides exist on the same day

gemini.text: The logic to increment retryCount is duplicated in the else block and the catch block - should be extracted into a helper function or restructured to avoid repetition
The prisma.workflowReminder.update call is duplicated - should be encapsulated into a dedicated function like incrementWorkflowReminderRetryCount(reminderId: number, currentRetryCount: number)

claude.text: Deletion query incorrectly deletes ALL workflow reminders (EMAIL, WHATSAPP, SMS) with retryCount > 1, not just SMS reminders. The second OR condition at lines 38-43 lacks a method filter, causing it to delete reminders across all communication channels when they exceed 1 retry attempt, violating the code comment which states the deletion should only affect scheduled sms reminders where scheduled date is past current date.

graphite.text: The second condition in the OR clause lacks the `method: WorkflowMethods.SMS` filter, causing ANY workflow reminder (email, webhook, etc.) with `retryCount > 1` to be deleted instead of just SMS reminders

qodo.text: The deleteMany filter deletes reminders with retryCount > 1 without scoping to SMS reminders, which could unintentionally delete non-SMS workflow reminders that have retryCount incremented

retryCount is updated via retryCount: reminder.retryCount + 1, which is susceptible to lost updates if multiple workers/processes pick up the same reminder concurrently - should use Prisma's atomic increment operation

retryCount is incremented both when scheduledSMS is falsy and when an exception is thrown - need to verify this matches intended semantics and that reminders won't be retried indefinitely between increments and the delete job timing

bugbot.text: The second OR condition in the delete query lacks the `method: WorkflowMethods.SMS` filter, causing the query to delete ALL workflow reminders with `retryCount > 1` regardless of method (EMAIL, WHATSAPP, etc.) instead of only SMS reminders

The `prisma.workflowReminder.update` call in the catch block is not wrapped in a try-catch, so if this database update fails the exception bubbles up unhandled, causing the handler to stop processing remaining reminders and the original error to never be logged

The `prisma.workflowReminder.update` call to increment `retryCount` is identically duplicated in two places (when `scheduledSMS` is falsy and in the catch block), risking inconsistent changes if the retry logic needs modification

copilot.text: The deletion query will delete ALL workflow reminders with retryCount > 1, regardless of their method type (WHATSAPP, EMAIL, etc.), even though retry logic is only being added for SMS reminders. The second OR condition should also check that the method is SMS.

When SMS scheduling conditions on line 158 are not met (message is empty/falsy or sendTo is missing), the reminder silently skips without incrementing the retry count, causing invalid reminders with missing data to keep getting processed on every cron run indefinitely.

WhatsApp reminder scheduling in scheduleWhatsappReminders.ts does not have similar retry logic, creating inconsistency where failed WhatsApp reminders will continue to retry indefinitely while SMS reminders are now limited to 2 retries.

Reminders that are missing workflowStep or booking data are silently skipped (via continue) without incrementing retryCount, causing these invalid reminders to be processed on every cron run indefinitely.

augment.text: The `retryCount > 1` branch isn't scoped to `method: SMS`, so it will delete any WorkflowReminder that happens to have retryCount incremented (even non-SMS methods), causing potential unintended data loss
`retryCount: reminder.retryCount + 1` is based on a potentially stale in-memory value; if this cron overlaps/runs concurrently it can lose increments
`update()` will throw if the reminder was deleted between `findMany` and this write, which would escape the catch block - needs to be robust to concurrency and missing rows

propel.text: Deleting reminders solely on retryCount > 1 without scoping to WorkflowMethods.SMS will remove retries for every workflow method (email, WhatsApp, etc.), even though this cron job only manages SMS reminders - the second branch of the OR should be restricted to SMS reminders

Using reminder.retryCount + 1 relies on the value read before scheduling, causing a race condition where concurrent requests can read the same retryCount and write the same new value, losing one increment - should use Prisma's atomic increment instead

Same race condition exists in the catch block where multiple schedulers failing simultaneously each write the same retryCount + 1, preventing the reminder from exceeding the threshold - should use atomic increment

baz.text: The cleanup deletes any workflow reminder with retryCount > 1 even if it is already scheduled or a non-SMS reminder, because retryCount isn't reset when a reminder finally schedules after multiple failures, causing the record to be immediately deleted before its scheduledDate

The prisma.workflowReminder.deleteMany where clause incorrectly deletes any reminder with retryCount > 1 even if it is already scheduled or not an SMS - should be limited to only delete SMS reminders and preserve scheduled items by using a condition like: method equals WorkflowMethods.SMS AND (scheduledDate <= now OR (scheduled is false AND retryCount > 1))

greptile.text: Deletion logic removes ALL reminders with retryCount > 1, not just SMS reminders. This will incorrectly delete email/whatsapp reminders that have been retried. Missing method: WorkflowMethods.SMS filter in the deletion query.

Retry count increments even for transient errors (network issues, temporary Twilio outages) that should be retried. Should consider only incrementing for permanent failures like invalid phone numbers.

Rate limit errors from checkSMSRateLimit will increment retry count, which may not be desired for quota-based failures. Should retry count only increment for permanent failures (invalid phone, locked account) vs transient errors (network, rate limits)?

kg.text: Delete condition removes any WorkflowReminder with retryCount > 1 regardless of method type, but retryCount is only incremented for SMS reminders - should add method: WorkflowMethods.SMS to the second OR condition to avoid unintended deletions

qodo-v2.text: scheduleSMSReminders deletes any WorkflowReminder with retryCount > 1 without restricting to method=SMS (or scheduled=false), risking unintended deletion of non-SMS reminders

devin.text: The second OR clause in the deleteMany query filters only on retryCount > 1 without any method constraint, causing the SMS cron job to delete all WorkflowReminder rows across all methods (EMAIL, WHATSAPP, SMS) that have retryCount > 1, not just SMS reminders. The fix is to add method: WorkflowMethods.SMS to the second OR branch.

greptile-v4.text: Missing method filter in retryCount deletion clause - the second OR branch deletes any WorkflowReminder with retryCount > 1 regardless of its method, which could silently delete non-SMS reminders

Catch block increments retryCount for unrelated errors - the catch block wraps the entire reminder-processing body including database lookups and template rendering, so any transient error will increment retryCount and potentially discard valid reminders after 2 failures even though Twilio was never contacted

Non-atomic retryCount increment - using reminder.retryCount + 1 reads from in-memory snapshot which can cause lost updates if two cron instances overlap, should use Prisma's atomic increment operator instead

mra-max.text: High churn and merge-driven instability around scheduleSMSReminders.ts due to multiple merge commits, causing unstable history and increased risk of reverts or missed changes
Deletion query in WhatsApp reminders incorrectly deletes non-WHATSAPP reminders because the second OR branch (retryCount > 1) has no method filter, potentially removing reminders from other channels
Core model/interface change to WorkflowReminder schema adds retryCount field which affects all consumers and queries that read or write WorkflowReminder records

Concurrency risk: potential race condition across cron workers scheduling the same reminders due to lack of cross-process locking or transactional guards, leading to possible duplicate Twilio calls or inconsistent state
Lack of tests around delete logic and cross-channel behavior increases risk of regressions as deletion logic is refactored
Schema change adds retryCount field to WorkflowReminder which propagates to all queries and code paths that read or update this field

mra-nano.text: CRON deleteMany removes non-SMS reminders due to missing method constraint - the retryCount > 1 clause does not constrain the method, causing unintended deletion of non-SMS reminders (emails/WhatsApp)
Possible runtime exception from reminder.booking?.startTime.toISOString() due to missing startTime - should use reminder.booking?.startTime?.toISOString() to safely handle missing startTime
Non-atomic retryCount increments when updating reminders - read-modify-write pattern creates race condition where concurrent updates could clobber each other, should use Prisma's atomic increment
retryCount type safety risk - PartialWorkflowReminder type omits retryCount, forcing unsafe casts and potential runtime misbehavior if retryCount is absent
deleteMany usage pattern exists in other reminder flows (e.g., scheduleEmailReminders.ts) suggesting broader data loss risk that warrants safety review
Inconsistent propagation of retryCount across callers - WhatsApp reminders flow doesn't fetch or update retryCount unlike SMS flow, leading to missed retry tracking
No index on retryCount in WorkflowReminder schema - deleteMany cleanup filtering by retryCount > 1 could require full table scan on large datasets

mra-ultra.text: Deletion query mis-scoped: retryCount > 1 clause deletes non-SMS reminders because it is not constrained by method, risking unintended data loss for non-SMS workflow reminders

Async side-effects not awaited in Email reminders: Promise.allSettled(deletePromises).then(...) is not awaited or returned from the handler, meaning deletion results are processed asynchronously and failures may be hidden
Schema migration adds NOT NULL retryCount column; if migration is not applied in all environments before new code is deployed, reads and writes to retryCount will fail

mra-b.text: deleteMany OR branch with retryCount > 1 has no constraint on method, causing any WorkflowReminder with retryCount > 1 to be deleted (including EMAIL, WHATSAPP, or future methods) instead of only SMS reminders as intended

mra-a.text: deleteMany OR clause with retryCount > 1 will delete any WorkflowReminder row regardless of method (including EMAIL reminders), not just SMS reminders as intended, causing silent data loss
deleteMany OR clause deletes SMS reminders immediately when retryCount > 1 even if they are scheduled in the future or have other state that might require retention
Migration adds NOT NULL DEFAULT column which can cause table rewrite and exclusive lock on large PostgreSQL tables, potentially causing long blocking times or downtime

entelligence.text: The new retryCount condition in the deleteMany query is not scoped to WorkflowMethods.SMS, causing deletion of failed reminders for all workflow methods (e.g., Email) instead of just SMS reminders. The method filter should be applied globally to the OR condition to prevent unintended data loss for non-SMS reminders.

sourcery.text: Deleting by retryCount > 1 without constraining by method or date may delete more reminders than intended - any WorkflowReminder with retryCount > 1 is now eligible for deletion, even if it's not SMS or its scheduledDate is in the future
Using reminder.retryCount + 1 risks lost updates when multiple workers/processes operate on the same reminder - should use Prisma's atomic operator instead (retryCount: { increment: 1 })

The deleteMany condition now deletes any WorkflowReminder with retryCount > 1 regardless of method - if the intent is to limit retries only for SMS reminders, consider scoping the retryCount condition under method: WorkflowMethods.SMS instead of using a top-level OR

The retry increment logic is duplicated in both the else branch (no scheduledSMS.sid) and the catch block - extracting this into a shared helper or function will reduce the risk of divergence and make future changes to the retry policy easier

cubic-dev.text: The `retryCount` update uses a stale in-memory value (`reminder.retryCount + 1`) instead of an atomic database increment. If two concurrent cron invocations process the same reminder, one increment is silently lost—meaning the reminder may never reach the deletion threshold. Use Prisma's atomic `{ increment: 1 }` instead.
The retry cleanup condition is missing an SMS method filter, so reminders from other workflow methods can be deleted when `retryCount > 1`.

mesa.text: The second OR branch `retryCount > 1` is not scoped to `method: WorkflowMethods.SMS`, causing deletion logic to delete non-SMS workflow reminders (email, webhook, etc.) once their retryCount exceeds 1, violating the function's domain boundary
Race condition issue at line 195 - retry count increment uses non-atomic read-modify-write pattern instead of atomic increment

Incrementing retryCount in the catch block consumes retry budget for all exception types including transient infrastructure errors (network timeouts, temporary service outages) that might succeed on retry - should classify errors as permanent vs retryable
Non-atomic retry increments at lines 184 and 195 create race conditions vulnerable to concurrent scheduler runs, leading to lost updates, incorrect counts, and premature or delayed deletions
Hard-coded retry threshold scattered in code lacks operational tunability and no exponential backoff or intelligent retry scheduling exists
Terminal failures result in silent deletion with only console.log visibility - no structured metrics, reason codes, dead-letter tracking, or audit trail for debugging systematic provider issues

codeant.text: Deletion condition unintentionally applies to all methods - the deleteMany where clause's retryCount branch does not restrict to SMS, so any WorkflowReminder of any method with retryCount > 1 will be deleted
Risk of race conditions when updating retryCount - concurrent cron executions might both try to increment retryCount; should use atomic increment instead of reading and writing back
Logging should include reminder.id and retryCount for easier debugging when errors occur
Missing logging when retryCount is incremented and when a reminder is deleted due to retryCount threshold

Missing unit/integration tests for: scheduling fails once (retryCount increments to 1), scheduling fails twice (retryCount increments to 2 and reminder removed), and ensuring other reminder methods are not deleted when retryCount > 1
Consider adding an index on retryCount if deleting by retryCount frequently

kodus.text: The deleteMany query incorrectly deletes non-SMS reminders because the OR condition allows any reminder with retryCount > 1 to be deleted regardless of its method - the method: WorkflowMethods.SMS condition should be moved outside the OR block to apply to both conditions

Table-locking DDL operation: Adding a column with both NOT NULL and DEFAULT value in a single statement triggers a full table rewrite and lock, causing application outage during deployment on large tables - should be broken into multiple non-locking migrations (add nullable column, backfill data in batches, then add NOT NULL constraint)

The deleteMany OR clause retryCount > 1 branch has no method filter, causing unintended deletion of non-SMS reminders (EMAIL, WHATSAPP) when their retryCount exceeds 1 - need to add method: WorkflowMethods.SMS to the retryCount OR branch

claude-code.text: The prisma.workflowReminder.update in the catch block (line 191) is unprotected - if it throws (e.g., the record was already deleted by the deleteMany above, or a DB connection issue), the error propagates out of the for...of loop and halts processing of all remaining reminders. Wrap this update in a nested try-catch or append .catch() to prevent a single failure from stopping the entire cron job.

kodus-v2.text: The deleteMany query in scheduleSMSReminders.ts uses an OR clause where the second branch (retryCount > 1) lacks a method: WorkflowMethods.SMS filter, causing every SMS cron invocation to delete all WorkflowReminder records with retryCount > 1 including EMAIL and WHATSAPP reminders, not just SMS ones

Duplicate retryCount increment: identical prisma.workflowReminder.update calls exist in both the else branch (when scheduledSMS.sid is falsy) and the catch block, potentially advancing retryCount by 2 in a single pass if the else-branch update throws, causing premature deletion since the cleanup query deletes rows with retryCount > 1

qodo-v2-2.text: scheduleSMSReminders deletes WorkflowReminder rows with retryCount > 1 without constraining method, so the SMS cron endpoint can delete EMAIL/WHATSAPP reminders if their retryCount ever becomes > 1, causing cross-method reminder loss

The catch block performs an awaited prisma.workflowReminder.update that can throw (e.g., if reminder already deleted), which will stop processing remaining reminders - a reliability regression since previously the catch only logged the error

qodo-v22.text: scheduleSMSReminders deletes any WorkflowReminder with retryCount > 1 because the deleteMany uses a top-level OR where the retryCount branch is not scoped to SMS, which can delete non-SMS reminders (EMAIL/WHATSAPP) if their retryCount is ever incremented

When twilioProvider.scheduleSMS returns undefined due to SMS sending being locked, scheduleSMSReminders treats it as a scheduling failure and increments retryCount, causing reminders to be deleted after two cron runs even though they were skipped intentionally due to a lock

retryCount is updated as reminder.retryCount + 1 using a value read earlier via findMany, so concurrent scheduler runs can overwrite each other and lose increments, making the two failures then delete behavior unreliable

qodo-extended.text: Non-atomic retry increment: retryCount is updated using a read-then-write (retryCount: reminder.retryCount + 1), which can lose increments if two cron executions update the same reminder between read and write

Unguarded update in catch block: The catch block awaits prisma.workflowReminder.update without handling failures; if the reminder is missing (e.g., deleted concurrently) the update can throw and abort processing subsequent reminders

Unscoped deletion deletes non-SMS reminders: The deleteMany OR condition's second branch { retryCount: { gt: 1 } } lacks a method: WorkflowMethods.SMS filter, so the SMS cron job will delete ALL WorkflowReminder records with retryCount > 1 regardless of method type (EMAIL, WHATSAPP)

No retry increment for invalid data: When message is empty/null or sendTo is falsy, the code falls through without incrementing retryCount, so reminders with permanently invalid data remain at retryCount: 0 and scheduled: false, causing them to be re-fetched and wastefully re-processed on every cron run

cubic-v2.text: The retryCount is incremented using the stale in-memory value (reminder.retryCount + 1) rather than an atomic database operation. If this cron handler runs concurrently or the same reminder is processed twice, both executions read the same retryCount and write the same incremented value, losing one update. Should use Prisma's atomic increment instead: retryCount: { increment: 1 }
The retry-count cleanup is not scoped to SMS reminders. As written, any reminder with retryCount > 1 will be deleted, including EMAIL and WHATSAPP rows

qodo-extended-summary.text: If Twilio scheduling is skipped because SMS sending is locked, twilioProvider.scheduleSMS returns undefined and scheduleSMSReminders increments retryCount as if scheduling failed, which can lead to reminders being deleted after two runs even though no scheduling attempt was made

The second OR branch { retryCount: { gt: 1 } } in the deleteMany query has no method filter, so it matches and deletes ALL WorkflowReminder records (EMAIL, WHATSAPP, SMS) with retryCount > 1, causing silent data loss of non-SMS reminders every time the SMS cron job runs
The catch block unconditionally calls prisma.workflowReminder.update, but if that update fails (e.g., row deleted concurrently), the error escapes the catch and can fail the whole cron run
Using retryCount: reminder.retryCount + 1 is a non-atomic read-modify-write that can lose increments under concurrent processing

gemini.text: Tests for caching behavior have been removed but the caching logic still exists in InsightsBookingService - these tests should be re-added to ensure caching functionality remains covered

The reduce implementation for joining filter conditions with AND in buildFilterConditions (lines 97-123) should use Prisma.join for better readability, and the OR condition for eventTypeId should be explicitly wrapped in parentheses to avoid SQL operator precedence issues
The reduce implementation for combining SQL conditions (lines 171-174) should use Prisma.join instead for more idiomatic and readable code
The reduce implementation for combining SQL conditions (lines 204-207) should use Prisma.join instead for more idiomatic and readable code

qodo.text: SQL injection / authorization scope: The new implementation constructs raw SQL conditions for authorization and filtering. While template interpolation via Prisma.sql should parameterize values, pay extra attention to ANY(${array}) bindings and any future changes that might interpolate untrusted strings/identifiers. Also re-validate that the OR-combination of team-scope and user-scope predicates cannot broaden access beyond the intended org/team membership rules.

Validate that using ANY(${someNumberArray}) inside Prisma.sql binds as a proper Postgres int[] (and not e.g. JSON/text), otherwise the ANY() predicates may behave incorrectly or error. Consider whether explicit casting or Prisma.join is needed depending on Prisma/PG behavior.

getBaseConditions() treats both auth and filter conditions as truthy values; since auth conditions always return a Prisma.Sql (including 1=0), verify the intended semantics when filters are absent and auth resolves to NOTHING_CONDITION, and ensure callers don't accidentally treat 1=0 as no condition rather than deny all.

Direct equality checks against Prisma.sql objects can be brittle if Prisma changes SQL formatting/parentheses/whitespace or parameter ordering. Consider asserting on behavior (query results) or normalizing the SQL representation rather than strict object equality for complex OR/AND conditions.

bugbot.text: The `else if (filterConditions)` and `else` branches in `getBaseConditions` are unreachable because `getAuthorizationConditions()` always returns a `Prisma.Sql` object (never null or undefined), so `authConditions` is always truthy, making lines 76-80 dead code

The same `reduce` pattern for joining `Prisma.Sql` conditions with `OR` is duplicated in `buildOrgAuthorizationCondition` and `buildTeamAuthorizationCondition`, and a similar pattern with `AND` exists in `buildFilterConditions` - this repetition could be extracted into a helper function like `joinSqlConditions(conditions: Prisma.Sql[], operator: 'AND' | 'OR')`

copilot.text: Constructor parameter type changed from InsightsBookingServiceOptions (validated via zod schema) to InsightsBookingServicePublicOptions (no runtime validation), which is inconsistent with InsightsRoutingService pattern and reduces type safety

InsightsBookingServicePublicOptions type appears unused and unnecessary - constructor accepts this type but validates against insightsBookingServiceOptionsSchema, creating confusion with two similar types

Caching tests were removed from the test suite but the service implementation still includes caching logic - caching behavior should continue to be tested to ensure it works correctly with Prisma.Sql objects

augment.text: userIdsFromOrg becomes empty when teamsFromOrg.length === 0, which would exclude org-member personal bookings for orgs that have no child teams - need to verify if this scenario is possible and whether org-scope auth should still include org-level memberships

Arrays used in ANY(...) (e.g. teamIds / uniqueUserIds) come from DB queries without explicit ordering, so the bound array order and resulting Prisma.sql shape may be non-deterministic, making SQL-fragment equality assertions brittle and causing inconsistent condition caching across identical inputs

baz.text: Public constructor allows scope='team' without requiring teamId, and safeParse nulls out invalid options, causing buildAuthorizationConditions to silently return NOTHING_CONDITION (1=0) instead of logging/throwing an error - this hides caller mistakes by making them look like empty booking sets

buildOrgAuthorizationCondition and buildTeamAuthorizationCondition repeat the same 6-line conditions.reduce-with-OR pattern, requiring changes in multiple places - should extract a reusable joinWithOr(conditions) helper function

greptile.text: teamIds is always non-empty (includes orgId), so the code will fail when passed an empty org with no teams because ANY(${[]}) creates invalid SQL

kg.text: Breaking API change - The findMany method has been removed from InsightsBookingService, replaced by getBaseConditions which returns a Prisma.Sql object. Existing code using findMany will break.

qodo-v2.text: buildOrgAuthorizationCondition only fetches org user IDs when teamsFromOrg.length > 0, even though org membership is represented via Membership on the org team ID itself. If an org has no child teams, the SQL will include only team bookings and will omit the user-booking branch for org members, leading to incomplete org-scope visibility.

Tests deep-compare Prisma.sql objects, but org/team authorization SQL embeds teamIds/userIds arrays derived from findMany queries without orderBy. That ordering is not guaranteed, so the produced Prisma.sql.values can vary between runs even if the SQL is semantically equivalent, causing flaky tests.

The constructor accepts a looser InsightsBookingServicePublicOptions where teamId is optional even when scope is 'team', so invalid option shapes compile and are only caught by runtime safeParse. On validation failure the service silently sets options=null and returns 1=0 authorization, which can turn a caller bug into confusing 'no data' behavior.
The integration test suite removed the caching tests but the service still relies on cachedAuthConditions/cachedFilterConditions. This reduces regression protection around caching semantics.

greptile-v4.text: getAuthorizationConditions() always returns a Prisma.Sql object (never null), making the else if (filterConditions) branch and final else branch unreachable dead code in getBaseConditions

Array.prototype.reduce called without initial value means index is never 0 inside the callback, making the if (index === 0) return condition branch unreachable dead code - appears in buildTeamAuthorizationCondition and buildOrgAuthorizationCondition
Type-safety regression: teamId is now optional for all scopes in InsightsBookingServicePublicOptions, meaning scope: 'team' without teamId compiles but fails at runtime instead of compile-time
Caching tests were removed but caching logic still exists in the service, creating a test coverage regression with no tests verifying cached values are returned on repeated calls
toEqual assertion on Prisma.Sql objects is order-sensitive but teamIds and userIds arrays are built from database queries without ORDER BY clause, causing potential test flakiness
When team has zero accepted members, the user-scope condition is completely omitted, which is a behavioral change from previous implementation with no test coverage for this edge case
Integration test queries BookingTimeStatusDenormalized without scoping to test-specific boundary, risking spurious failures from concurrent test data
Cleanup block in integration test won't execute if assertion throws, leaving userBooking and userEventType in the database - needs try/finally wrapper

mra-max.text: Large churn in InsightsBookingService refactor to generate SQL via Prisma.sql, moving away from object-based Prisma inputs, introduces instability and potential regressions
Tests that validate raw SQL fragments and rely on NOTHING_CONDITION and Prisma.sql templates introduce instability
Authorization and filtering logic may have regressions due to the refactor
Test suite shows high churn and reliance on DB-backed integration tests, raising flakiness risk in CI
Caching of authorization and filter SQL fragments may become stale when options or filters change
Raw SQL with array parameters must be consistently parameterized to avoid issues
CI integration details (Postgres service, test database, secrets) contribute to environmental fragility
Reverts/merge instability in history indicates potential issues with the changes

mra-nano.text: InsightsBookingService has been substantially refactored from object-based Prisma where inputs to Prisma.sql template literals for all condition builders, with integration tests overhauled to assert against SQL fragments rather than object structures - this is a high-churn change spanning core authorization, filter, and base condition construction

Inconsistent NOTHING sentinel pattern between InsightsBookingService (uses SQL-based sentinel Prisma.sql`1=0`) and InsightsRoutingService (uses plain object { id: -1 }), creating divergent semantics for the 'no conditions' case across services which risks subtle bugs when code paths from both services are compared or unified

mra-ultra.text: NOTHING_CONDITION is a module-level singleton with mutable state that could be corrupted when reused across multiple service instances or calls due to Prisma.sql template tag mutations

Constructor silently sets options to null on validation failure instead of throwing an error, causing callers to receive empty results (1=0) without any warning when passing invalid options like scope: 'team' without teamId

Team-scope query silently excludes individual non-team bookings when team has zero accepted members, as the userId clause is skipped entirely when userIdsFromTeam is empty - a behavioral change from the old code
Org-scope authorization has the same issue where user bookings are excluded when userIdsFromOrg is empty
Test file defines its own NOTHING_CONDITION separately from the service's instance, and toEqual comparisons on Prisma.Sql objects may be unreliable due to internal structure differences

Org-level authorization always includes orgId in teamIds, but userIdsFromOrg query only fetches users when teamsFromOrg.length > 0, creating asymmetry where team bookings for org are included but user bookings are excluded if org has direct members but no child teams

mra-b.text: Caching behavior is implemented in the service using cachedAuthConditions and cachedFilterConditions, but the integration tests that previously verified memoization behavior were removed, leaving no tests to verify caching works correctly or detect regressions

InsightsBookingService.findMany method was removed and replaced with getBaseConditions(), which is a breaking public API change that will cause compile/runtime failures for any external callers that previously used findMany

Raw Prisma.Sql fragment comparison and interpolation in tests is brittle - tests compare Prisma.Sql objects via deep equality and embed them in raw $queryRaw calls, which depends on Prisma's internal Sql representation and may break across versions or produce false results if parameter binding semantics change

mra-a.text: Tests assert deep equality between separately constructed Prisma.sql instances, which may not hold across Prisma versions due to differing internal object shapes or parameter metadata
Interpolating a Prisma.Sql fragment into a $queryRaw tagged template may not be supported as intended in all runtime environments, risking incorrect SQL execution or runtime errors

sourcery.text: getBaseConditions contains unreachable else branches (else if (filterConditions) and final else) because getAuthorizationConditions always returns a Prisma.Sql (defaulting to NOTHING_CONDITION), so authConditions is never falsy. Either allow getAuthorizationConditions to return null to make these branches meaningful, or simplify getBaseConditions to assume authConditions is always present.

cubic-dev.text: `getBaseConditions` has two unreachable branches. `getAuthorizationConditions()` always returns a `Prisma.Sql` object (never null), so `authConditions` is always truthy. The `else if (filterConditions)` and `else` branches can never execute. Consider simplifying to just two cases: filter present or absent.

Caching tests were deleted instead of being converted to the new `Prisma.sql` format. The service still has `cachedAuthConditions` / `cachedFilterConditions` fields and memoization logic that is now untested at the integration level. These tests are trivial to keep — they only verify `conditions1 === conditions2` across repeated calls and don't depend on the assertion format.

mesa.text: Removal of findMany and replacement with getBaseConditions breaks service encapsulation - callers now need to know how to construct $queryRaw queries and understand the table structure
Service layer API inconsistency - InsightsRoutingService maintains a findMany method that encapsulates data access while InsightsBookingService now forces callers to handle raw query execution
Test code performs raw SQL queries directly which undermines service layer encapsulation - integration tests should call service methods that return results, not conditions

Type safety regression - switching from Prisma.BookingTimeStatusDenormalizedWhereInput to raw Prisma.sql templates loses compile-time schema validation, typos in column names won't be caught until runtime
Integration tests validate exact SQL structure rather than query behavior, coupling them to implementation details and making them fragile to benign formatting changes

codeant.text: Manual reduce to combine SQL fragments is error-prone - should use Prisma.sql.join for clarity and to avoid accidental parenthesis mistakes
ANY with empty arrays yields false for all comparisons - need to handle edge case properly (already handled for userId but should verify for all cases)

Comparing Prisma.sql objects directly in tests is brittle - two separately created Prisma.sql template literals may not be equal even with same contents. Should compare serialized SQL and parameters instead
Double-quoted column names in raw SQL must match actual database column names - if Prisma mapping uses different underlying column names or database uses snake_case, queries will break
Test assertions using deep equality against inline Prisma.sql template literals may fail if service constructs SQL with slightly different parenthesis arrangement even if semantically equivalent
Missing explicit unit/integration test for empty team/user list cases to verify OR branch doesn't include ANY(...) for empty arrays

kodus.text: Logical error in authorization condition - the org-scope authorization condition incorrectly includes the organization's ID in the list of values to be compared with the teamId column. An organization ID is not a team ID, making this comparison logically incorrect. This adds a useless condition to the query and could lead to incorrect results if an ID collision between an organization and a team were to occur.

claude-code.text: SQL operator precedence bug: buildOrgAuthorizationCondition and buildTeamAuthorizationCondition produce top-level OR expressions without outer parentheses. When getBaseConditions() returns these directly (line 75, no filters), callers composing with AND get incorrect precedence - AND binds tighter than OR, so team bookings bypass additional filters entirely. Fix: wrap the return at line 75 with parens around authConditions, or add outer parens in the reduce calls.
Dead code: since authConditions is always a Prisma.Sql object (never null), the else if (filterConditions) and else branches (lines 76-80) are unreachable dead code

Constructor parameter type was changed from InsightsBookingServiceOptions (discriminated union requiring teamId when scope: 'team') to InsightsBookingServicePublicOptions (flat type with teamId?: number), which loses compile-time enforcement that teamId is provided for team scope. While runtime zod validation catches this, the failure mode is silent empty results instead of a compile error. This is inconsistent with InsightsRoutingService which still uses the strict discriminated union type.

qodo-v2-2.text: InsightsBookingService accepts an options type where teamId is optional even when scope is 'team', allowing callers to compile with invalid team options. The constructor silently treats them as invalid and returns 1=0 authorization conditions, turning programmer errors into empty results without explicit failure, making team-scoped insights unexpectedly return nothing and hard to debug.

qodo-v22.text: InsightsBookingServicePublicOptions makes teamId optional even when scope is 'team', but the runtime zod schema requires teamId for team scope. This allows callers to compile invalid 'team' options and then get empty results at runtime because validation fails and authorization falls back to NOTHING_CONDITION

qodo-extended.text: InsightsBookingService now accepts InsightsBookingServicePublicOptions where teamId is optional, allowing scope: 'team' calls without teamId to compile but be rejected at runtime and return NOTHING_CONDITION (empty results), turning a compile-time guaranteed contract into a silent runtime failure

JavaScript arrays are passed directly to Prisma.sql template literals with PostgreSQL's ANY() operator, but Prisma.sql serializes interpolated values as individual scalar parameters, not as PostgreSQL array types required by ANY(), which will produce a runtime SQL type error

The entire 'Caching' describe block with tests validating that getAuthorizationConditions() and getFilterConditions() return cached results on repeated calls is deleted, while the caching logic remains active in the service, leaving the caching mechanism untested

Test expectations use toEqual() to compare Prisma.Sql objects, but the service constructs SQL via nested conditions.reduce() producing nested Prisma.Sql wrappers, while tests use flat single template literals, which may cause tests to spuriously pass or fail depending on Prisma.Sql internal representation

cubic-v2.text: Org-scope assertion is order-dependent even though the service fetches team/member IDs without orderBy, so the test can fail nondeterministically on valid SQL

getAuthorizationConditions() always returns a Prisma.Sql object (never null/undefined), so authConditions is always truthy and the else if (filterConditions) and else branches are unreachable dead code - should simplify to just two branches or add explicit nullability path if intended

qodo-extended-summary.text: The service's conditions.reduce() pattern in buildTeamAuthorizationCondition, buildOrgAuthorizationCondition, and buildFilterConditions produces nested Prisma.Sql objects where .values contains inner Prisma.Sql instances, but test expectations use flat Prisma.sql template literals with primitive .values, causing at least 4 test cases to fail due to structural mismatch under deep equality comparison

The entire Caching describe block (2 tests verifying that getAuthorizationConditions() and getFilterConditions() return cached results on repeated calls) was deleted without replacement, while the caching logic (cachedAuthConditions/cachedFilterConditions with undefined-checks) remains in the service with zero test coverage

gemini.text: Direct string comparison of webhook secret is vulnerable to timing attacks - should use constant-time comparison function like crypto.timingSafeEqual

refreshOAuthTokens function has inconsistent return types - returns fetch Response object when app credential syncing is enabled but returns refreshFunction result (which varies by integration) when not enabled, causing runtime errors

Operations in app-credential.ts (Zod parsing, decryption, JSON parsing) can throw unhandled exceptions resulting in 500 Internal Server Error - should wrap in try/catch and return appropriate error response
minimumTokenResponseSchema is too broad - the pattern [z.string().toString()]: z.number() matches any numeric property as expiry time, which could incorrectly match fields like user_id

parseRefreshTokenResponse incorrectly sets refresh_token to literal string 'refresh_token' when not present in response - this will cause future token refresh attempts to fail and should remain undefined instead

claude.text: Salesforce integration uses stale credentials after token refresh - after refreshing tokens and updating the database with new tokens from accessTokenParsed.data, the code creates a jsforce.Connection using the OLD credentialKey values instead of the newly refreshed tokens, causing immediate API failures

Salesforce integration missing refreshOAuthTokens wrapper - unlike all other integrations (Google Calendar, Office365, Zoom, Hubspot, etc.), Salesforce makes a direct fetch() call instead of using the refreshOAuthTokens() wrapper, which means it will bypass the custom sync endpoint even when APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED is true
Zoho-Bigin passes wrong parameter type to refreshOAuthTokens - passes credentialId (credential's database ID) instead of credential.userId, causing the sync endpoint to receive the wrong identifier

Invalid Zod schema syntax in parseRefreshTokenResponse - uses z.string().toString() as object keys, which evaluates to the literal string '[object Object]' instead of creating dynamic key validation, causing token validation to fail for credential sync mode

Google Calendar stores entire parse result instead of extracted data - stores the entire SafeParseReturnType object {success: boolean, data: any} to the database instead of extracting .data like other integrations (Zoom, Office365, Salesforce)

Hardcoded refresh_token placeholder breaks token preservation - sets refresh_token to literal string 'refresh_token' when missing, contradicting commit 358aa8be2's pattern to preserve old tokens, causing OAuth providers to reject this invalid token string

graphite.text: Invalid Zod schema syntax - the syntax `[z.string().toString()]: z.number()` attempts to use dynamic computed keys which Zod doesn't support, causing a runtime error
Should use `.passthrough()` to allow additional properties instead of trying to define dynamic keys

The webhook endpoint should use constant-time compare for secret validation
The webhook endpoint needs abuse protections including method restriction, size limits, rate limiting, and replay protection since it can overwrite credentials if the secret leaks

refreshOAuthTokens can return a fetch Response when credential syncing is enabled, but callers expect different shapes (axios responses with .data, or provider SDK token objects), which can break token refresh flows at runtime

The Zod schema uses computed keys via [z.string().toString()] which does not behave like an index signature and won't validate responses as intended - should use z.record(...) or .passthrough() instead
The schema needs to explicitly model expires_in/expiry_date and refresh_token handling
The webhook handler lacks request method validation
The webhook handler doesn't normalize header value types (string vs string[])

bugbot.text: Google Calendar stores SafeParseReturnType object instead of extracting key.data, corrupting stored credentials and breaking future token refreshes
Zoho Bigin passes credentialId instead of userId to refreshOAuthTokens function, breaking credential sync functionality
minimumTokenResponseSchema uses computed property syntax [z.string().toString()] which evaluates to literal string key instead of pattern matcher
