copilot.text: refreshOAuthTokens may return a fetch Response when sync is enabled, but code assumes an axios response (zohoCrmTokenInfo.data...), which will throw at runtime when syncing is turned on
Handler doesn't restrict HTTP methods; it will process GET/PUT/etc. Should enforce POST and return 405 otherwise to align with webhook semantics
Sync request to CALCOM_CREDENTIAL_SYNC_ENDPOINT is unauthenticated (no shared-secret header) and doesn't set a content type, risking token exfiltration via SSRF/misconfig
minimumTokenResponseSchema computed keys like [z.string().toString()] become literal string keys at build time, not index signatures, so schema won't validate arbitrary expiry fields
Setting a missing refresh_token to the literal string 'refresh_token' will overwrite valid stored refresh tokens in callers when they update DB with newTokens.refresh_token
When sync is enabled, refreshOAuthTokens returns a fetch Response, but Google calendar code treats return value as gaxios response with .data property, causing token refresh to fail in sync mode
Third argument to refreshOAuthTokens is userId, but code passes credentialId (credential primary key), sending wrong identifier to sync endpoint and bypassing userId truthiness check
prisma is used but isn't imported in the file, which will fail at build time
appCredentialWebhookRequestBodySchema.parse(req.body) will throw and return 500 on invalid input; for public webhook endpoint should use safeParse and return 400/422 with validation error
In sync mode refreshOAuthTokens returns a fetch Response, but HubSpot code expects a HubspotToken object and immediately reads expiresIn, accessToken, etc., which will fail at runtime
Header values in req.headers[...] can be string | string[] | undefined; directly comparing to CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET will fail if header is an array and can be bypassed with multiple values
Salesforce token refresh uses response.statusText !== 'OK' for error handling; statusText is not reliable across runtimes and proxies, should use response.ok / response.status instead
Credential update replaces key with refreshed response, but in credential-sync mode response is minimal, risking dropping required Salesforce key fields like instance_url, id, issued_at
APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED is currently a string (encryption key) rather than boolean, making flag's type misleading and causing potential truthy check or type issues

refreshOAuthTokens can return Response from fetch when syncing enabled, but returns whatever refreshFunction() returns otherwise (axios response, HubSpot token object, gaxios response), and call sites assume specific shapes, so enabling sync will break integrations

augment.text: minimumTokenResponseSchema uses computed keys ([z.string().toString()]), which will literally create keys like "[object Object]" and won't validate arbitrary numeric expiry fields as intended; safeParse will likely fail for real token responses

parseRefreshTokenResponse switches to minimumTokenResponseSchema whenever credential sharing + sync endpoint are configured, even when the refresh call is still hitting the third-party provider (e.g., team credentials), so valid provider responses can be parsed against the wrong schema
Setting a missing refresh_token to the literal string "refresh_token" can overwrite a previously valid refresh token when callers merge/store .data, which would break subsequent refreshes

refreshOAuthTokens returns a fetch Response when syncing is enabled but returns whatever refreshFunction() returns otherwise (axios/hubspot/google responses), so downstream code can break depending on env
res?.data assumes a Gaxios-style response, but when refreshOAuthTokens uses fetch it returns a Response without .data, so token.access_token/token.expiry_date will be undefined and refresh will fail
parseRefreshTokenResponse returns a Zod safeParse result object, so data: { key } would persist { success, data, ... } instead of the credential JSON payload
When syncing is enabled, refreshOAuthTokens returns a Response, but this code treats it as a HubspotToken and immediately reads expiresIn/accessToken, which will throw at runtime
refreshOAuthTokens's third argument is userId, but this call passes credentialId; if syncing is enabled it will POST the wrong calcomUserId and likely refresh the wrong account

appCredentialWebhookRequestBodySchema.parse (and the subsequent JSON.parse) can throw and return a 500 for malformed requests; without a method guard this endpoint is also callable via non-POST methods
APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED resolves to a string/undefined (due to &&), not a boolean; any code that expects a strict boolean may behave unexpectedly
After refreshing and storing the new Salesforce access token, the jsforce.Connection is still initialized from the pre-refresh credentialKey values, so immediate API calls may use an expired token

propel.text: When APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED is true, the minimumTokenResponseSchema uses [z.string().toString()] which creates invalid computed property names (like 'function String() { [native code] }'), causing every real token response to fail safeParse and throwing 'Invalid refreshed tokens were returned'
The schema should use .catchall(z.unknown()) instead of computed property names to accept arbitrary keys (e.g. expires_in, expiry_date, provider-specific fields) while keeping access_token mandatory

baz.text: minimumTokenResponseSchema defines literal keys via [z.string().toString()] instead of allowing unknown keys, so the schema rejects normal refresh responses (which include expires_in, refresh_token, token_type, etc.), causing credential sync to throw 'Invalid refreshed tokens were returned'; should use .passthrough() or explicit field definitions

When refreshed response omits refresh_token, the code forces data.refresh_token = 'refresh_token' placeholder, and both Zoom/Office365 immediately write that placeholder over the credential row, so the real refresh_token is lost and all future refreshes fail; should preserve the existing token instead

refreshOAuthTokens now returns a raw fetch Response when credential sharing is enabled, but the handler treats the result as a gaxios response (res?.data) and reads token.access_token/expiry_date without calling res.json(), so the fields are undefined and refreshed tokens are never persisted

refreshOAuthTokens call supplies credentialId, so the sync post uses calcomUserId=credentialId even though refreshOAuthTokens builds the payload from the Cal.com userId, meaning the endpoint will not load the right user's tokens; should pass credential.userId instead

coderabbit.text: Handler accepts any HTTP method but should enforce POST with a 405 response like other webhook handlers
Header lookup is case-sensitive - if CALCOM_WEBHOOK_HEADER_NAME is set in mixed case, lookup will fail because Node.js normalizes incoming headers to lowercase
Header lookup doesn't handle arrays - if the header is sent multiple times, req.headers[...] will be an array and comparison against a string will incorrectly allow the request through
appCredentialWebhookRequestBodySchema.parse can throw on invalid payload, turning bad input into 500s instead of 400s
JSON.parse(symmetricDecrypt(...)) can throw on decrypt/JSON errors, turning bad input into 500s instead of 400s
minimumTokenResponseSchema uses [z.string().toString()] which yields a literal key and makes it required, so real token payloads will fail validation
parseRefreshTokenResponse overwrites missing refresh tokens with placeholder 'refresh_token' string, which will clobber valid refresh tokens downstream and break future refreshes
refreshOAuthTokens returns a Response object when credential sharing is enabled but returns refreshFunction() result otherwise - callers expect .data property which doesn't exist on Response objects
No error handling for the fetch call in refreshOAuthTokens - network failures throw unhandled exceptions
No timeout on the external fetch in refreshOAuthTokens, risking indefinite hangs
The () => any type signature in refreshOAuthTokens provides no type safety
No response validation in refreshOAuthTokens - the fetch response status and content aren't checked
Missing prisma import in salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts for credential update
zoho-bigin/lib/CalendarService.ts passes credentialId instead of credential.userId to refreshOAuthTokens, causing sync to target wrong subject
zohocrm/lib/CalendarService.ts caller expects AxiosResponse with .data property but refreshOAuthTokens can return fetch Response, causing runtime error
Read-then-write pattern for credential upsert can create duplicates under concurrent requests without unique constraint
office365calendar/lib/CalendarService.ts silently persists stale credentials if token parsing fails instead of handling the error
salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts uses stale credentialKey values when creating jsforce connection instead of refreshed token values
googlecalendar/lib/CalendarService.ts stores entire parseRefreshTokenResponse wrapper object as credential key instead of extracting .data property

greptile.text: Webhook authentication is vulnerable to timing attacks. String comparison allows attackers to brute-force the secret by measuring response times. Use constant-time comparison or a crypto library's compare function.
Missing HTTP method validation - only POST should be allowed for the webhook endpoint

Decryption will fail silently if the encryption key env var is not set (empty string fallback). This creates credentials with corrupt data instead of failing fast. Check that the key is configured before attempting decryption.

Missing error handling for JSON.parse and symmetricDecrypt. If decryption fails or returns invalid JSON, the endpoint will throw an unhandled error exposing stack traces. Wrap in try-catch and return 400.
The zod schema is invalid - computed property names with [z.string().toString()] don't work as zod schema definitions. This will cause runtime errors. Use z.record() for dynamic keys instead.

Setting a dummy 'refresh_token' string when missing will cause token refresh to fail on subsequent requests. This creates silent failures. Either fail fast here or document why this placeholder is acceptable.

Missing error handling for the fetch request. If CALCOM_CREDENTIAL_SYNC_ENDPOINT is unreachable or returns an error, this will fail silently. Add try-catch and fallback to refreshFunction() on failure.

The response from CALCOM_CREDENTIAL_SYNC_ENDPOINT is returned directly without validating the response format or checking HTTP status. Downstream code expects specific token structures - validate before returning.
Missing error handling for JSON.parse in decodeOAuthState. If req.query.state contains invalid JSON, this will throw an unhandled error. Wrap in try-catch and return undefined on parse failure.
encodeOAuthState function parses JSON and immediately stringifies it back - no actual encoding happens. The function name is misleading and this appears to be a no-op transformation.

qodo-v2.text: When delegation is enabled, refreshOAuthTokens returns a fetch Response, but multiple integrations expect axios/Gaxios/SDK token objects (with .data / token fields), causing runtime errors and preventing token refresh when CALCOM_CREDENTIAL_SYNC_ENDPOINT is set

parseRefreshTokenResponse's minimumTokenResponseSchema is effectively just {access_token}, causing expiry/refresh_token fields to be stripped under delegation, and it injects a dummy refresh_token value which can overwrite real refresh tokens and produce NaN expiry computations

GoogleCalendarService stores the Zod safeParse result object into credential.key instead of the credential JSON shape, and delegated refresh returns a fetch Response so res.data is undefined, causing token updates to fail

Zoho Bigin passes credentialId into refreshOAuthTokens where a Cal.com userId is expected, so delegated refresh will call the external endpoint with the wrong identifier, preventing the external app store from locating the correct user tokens
SalesforceCalendarService uses prisma without importing it and instantiates jsforce.Connection using the old access token even after refreshing and storing new tokens
Salesforce refresh checks response.statusText instead of response.ok, which is unreliable

The webhook handler does not enforce POST method and performs zod.parse, symmetricDecrypt, and JSON.parse without try/catch, causing invalid payloads or misconfiguration to throw 500s instead of clean 4xx errors

devin.text: Google Calendar stores SafeParseResult wrapper {success, data} as credential key instead of just the parsed data - parseRefreshTokenResponse returns the full SafeParseReturnType object but the code stores it directly, causing credential loading to fail after token refresh

Zoho Bigin passes credential database ID instead of user ID to refreshOAuthTokens - credentialId (credential row's primary key) is passed where userId is expected, causing sync endpoint to look up wrong user

Hubspot refreshOAuthTokens returns raw fetch Response when credential sharing is enabled, but caller expects HubspotToken - accessing .expiresIn and .accessToken on Response object returns undefined, corrupting stored credentials
Same Response type mismatch affects Zoho Bigin and Zoho CRM which use axios expecting AxiosResponse with .data property

minimumTokenResponseSchema computed property keys resolve to literal string '[object Object]' instead of dynamic validators - z.string().toString() returns '[object Object]', making the schema only validate access_token while losing expiry date validation

greptile-v4.text: credentialId passed instead of userId to refreshOAuthTokens - the third parameter should be userId but credential's database ID is passed instead, causing wrong user lookup when credential sync endpoint is enabled
Zod's .parse() throws ZodError for malformed request body causing unhandled 500 instead of 400 - should use .safeParse() instead
symmetricDecrypt() and JSON.parse() can throw for wrong encryption key, malformed ciphertext, or invalid JSON, causing unhandled 500 instead of 400/422

Hardcoded placeholder string 'refresh_token' used as token value when response doesn't include refresh_token - downstream code will fail with opaque error when trying to use this non-functional placeholder

Broken Zod schema using computed property keys - Zod object as computed property key calls .toString() at definition time producing fixed literal string key, not dynamic key validator, causing second assignment to silently override first
Unreliable HTTP error check via response.statusText which varies between implementations - should use response.ok or response.status instead
Dead code - accessTokenParsed.success check is unreachable because parseRefreshTokenResponse throws on parse failure and only returns on success
Missing HTTP method guard - handler accepts every HTTP verb so GET/DELETE requests with correct secret would create or overwrite credentials
res.data is undefined in credential-sync path - refreshOAuthTokens returns raw fetch Response which has no .data property, causing TypeError when accessing token.access_token
Same res.data issue affects hubspot/lib/CalendarService.ts - hubspotRefreshToken.expiresIn becomes undefined causing NaN stored as credential expiry
Same res.data issue affects zohocrm/lib/CalendarService.ts - zohoCrmTokenInfo.data is undefined
openssl rand -base64 24 generates 24-byte key not 32 bytes required for AES-256 - should use openssl rand -base64 32

mra-max.text: refreshOAuthTokens returns raw Response object but callers inconsistently treat it as Axios-like object with .data field or as fetch Response, causing runtime errors depending on code path
Webhook decrypts payload with symmetricDecrypt and JSON.parse without try/catch or validation, causing unhandled exceptions and potential processing of tampered payloads
Missing CHANGELOG.md entry for breaking change that requires public documentation
Webhook credential sync endpoint relies solely on header secret without nonce, timestamp, or replay protection, creating credential manipulation risk if secret is leaked
parseRefreshTokenResponse sets refresh_token to literal string 'refresh_token' as default when provider omits it, propagating invalid tokens through the system
APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED is defined as boolean expression using environment variables, causing confusion and potential bugs with empty strings or non-boolean values
Encryption key length guidance mismatch in .env.example - comment says 32 bytes for AES256 but example shows 24-byte generation command
No tests added for new webhook/credential-sync flow
Missing documentation for webhook credential sync and token refresh flow in README or docs folder
External credential sync endpoint posts to external endpoint without strict input validation of response, potentially accepting malformed tokens
Token refresh wrappers assume specific response shapes from external endpoints; schema drift could break multiple app integrations (googlecalendar, hubspot, office365, etc.)
APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED gate derived from env presence could be misconfigured if vars are missing or overridden, enabling/disabling functionality inadvertently

New environment variables for credential sync (CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET, CALCOM_WEBHOOK_HEADER_NAME, CALCOM_CREDENTIAL_SYNC_ENDPOINT, CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY) expand configuration surface area and require proper docs
Potential unhandled promise rejections in async token refresh flows that may not propagate errors to callers in all code paths

mra-nano.text: New webhook endpoint api/webhook/app-credential has high-risk churn with credential syncing gated on APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED and header-secret check, touching credential state and user/app metadata with potential for misconfig or race conditions

refreshOAuthTokens returns raw Response object from fetch without parsing JSON when using credential sync endpoint, causing downstream code expecting res?.data to get undefined for access_token/expiry, breaking token refresh in self-hosted scenarios
APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED is defined as logical AND of two env vars resulting in non-boolean value relying on truthiness, which is brittle if env values are empty or misinterpreted
Encryption algorithm name 'aes256' is invalid - Node.js crypto expects specific names like 'aes-256-cbc' or 'aes-256-ctr', will likely throw at runtime or produce broken encryption

symmetricEncrypt/symmetricDecrypt use Buffer.from(key, 'latin1') but CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY is intended to be base64-encoded, so decoding with latin1 will not restore the original 32-byte key
No explicit validated key length check for AES-256 encryption key, allowing misconfiguration to silently break crypto or degrade security

New environment variables CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET, CALCOM_WEBHOOK_HEADER_NAME, CALCOM_CREDENTIAL_SYNC_ENDPOINT, CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY require correct configuration in self-hosted deployments
Env example documents encryption key must be 32 bytes for AES-256 but suggests 'openssl rand -base64 24' which produces 24 random bytes, not guaranteeing 32-byte key when decoded

New zod-based parseRefreshTokenResponse and refreshOAuthTokens helpers increase path complexity in calendar integrations and may cause runtime differences if token response shapes deviate from prior expectations

mra-ultra.text: parseRefreshTokenResponse returns the full Zod safeParse result (containing success and data fields) rather than the parsed token data itself, causing invalid shape to be stored in database and breaking subsequent token usage

refreshOAuthTokens wrapper returns inconsistent shape (Response vs JSON) - conditionally returns raw response from fetch/axios when callers expect a token object with properties like access_token and expiry_date, breaking token refresh flows for Google Calendar, Office 365, Zoom, etc.
Encryption code uses AES-256 CBC without integrity check (no MAC/HMAC), meaning ciphertext could be tampered with without detection, potentially leading to invalid decryptions or credential leakage

Credential Prisma model does not declare a unique constraint on the composite (userId, appId) key, allowing multiple credentials to exist for the same user and app, undermining canonical credential per user-app

Documentation in .env.example incorrectly specifies CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY length - states 32 bytes required for AES-256 but suggested command uses openssl rand -base64 24 which yields only 24 raw bytes
Webhook header name handling may fail if env defines non-lowercase header - req.headers lookup doesn't normalize header name to lower-case, causing potential 403s from misconfiguration

mra-b.text: Missing or unresolved symmetricDecrypt implementation — the import from @calcom/lib/crypto will resolve to undefined or fail to resolve at build time, causing the webhook to crash when invoked

parseRefreshTokenResponse's minimumTokenResponseSchema uses computed zod expressions (z.string().toString()) as object keys, which returns debugging strings instead of schema keys, causing the schema to not validate real token responses correctly

refreshOAuthTokens changes return contract — returns different shapes (fetch Response vs axios response/token object) depending on runtime env, breaking callers that expect specific shapes like .data property

GoogleCalendarService stores the entire SafeParse return object into DB instead of the parsed key object, corrupting the credential.key field with { success: true, data: { ... } } instead of the raw credential key object
Symmetric crypto usage is unsafe — uses bare AES cipher without authentication (no HMAC or AEAD mode like AES-GCM), allowing ciphertext tampering and potential padding oracle attacks
Symmetric crypto key encoding mismatch — key is decoded with latin1 but documentation suggests base64 generation, causing potential invalid key sizes or developer confusion
APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED evaluates to a string or undefined rather than a boolean, causing confusion and type-safety issues in TypeScript

mra-a.text: Webhook handler doesn't validate webhook encryption key before decrypting - if CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY is missing or malformed, symmetricDecrypt is called with empty string fallback, causing 500s and possible lost webhook deliveries

Malformed Zod schema in parseRefreshTokenResponse uses computed keys based on z.string().toString() which creates literal string keys like 'z.string()' instead of matching expected token fields like expiry_date or expires_in, causing validation failures

refreshOAuthTokens returns inconsistent types - sometimes returns refreshFunction's return value (axios response, library object, or string) and sometimes returns node-fetch Response, breaking callers that expect specific shapes

parseRefreshTokenResponse returns Zod SafeParseReturn object but code stores it directly to DB via prisma.credential.update, corrupting credential structure by storing wrapper object instead of parsed data

Salesforce CalendarService getClient constructs jsforce.Connection using stale credentialKey values instead of newly refreshed accessTokenParsed.data after token refresh, causing authentication errors

.env.example recommends 'openssl rand -base64 24' for AES-256 key but this produces incorrect length (should be 32 bytes), and webhook handler has no runtime length/format validation for encryption key

entelligence.text: The refreshOAuthTokens utility returns the result of the closure to all concurrent callers, but since a Response body can only be consumed once, calling resp.json() in handleLarkError will fail with a 'body already read' error for subsequent callers. The closure should parse the response and return the data instead.

prisma is referenced on line 96 but is not imported or defined in the file, which will throw a ReferenceError at runtime and fail token refresh. Need to import prisma from @calcom/prisma before calling prisma.credential.update.

sourcery.text: The `minimumTokenResponseSchema` object shape is invalid - using `[z.string().toString()]` as computed keys creates a single property with that literal key (e.g. 'ZodString'), not an index signature over arbitrary keys. Should use `z.object({ access_token: z.string() }).passthrough()` and handle expiry fields separately.

Setting a dummy `refresh_token` string ('refresh_token') when it is absent is risky and can corrupt credentials, causing subsequent refresh attempts to fail in a non-obvious way. Should avoid mutating and rely on callers to preserve existing refresh token.

`parseRefreshTokenResponse` returns a wrapper object (`{ success, data }`) but is used where `googleCredentialSchema.parse` was, which returned the parsed credential object directly. This will write the wrapper into `credential.key`, breaking callers that expect it to match `googleCredentialSchema`.

Mixing response types between the sync endpoint and `refreshFunction` will break callers - returns a `fetch` `Response` when sync is enabled but Axios/SDK results otherwise. Callers expecting `.data` or specific shapes will hit runtime errors when sync-path returns bare `Response`.

cubic-dev.text: `prisma` is used but never imported in packages/app-store/salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts. This will throw `ReferenceError: prisma is not defined` at runtime. Add `import prisma from "@calcom/prisma";` to the imports.

`credentialId` (the credential's database primary key) is passed as the `userId` parameter of `refreshOAuthTokens` in packages/app-store/zoho-bigin/lib/CalendarService.ts, which forwards it to the sync endpoint as `calcomUserId`. This sends the wrong identifier to the credential sync service. The user's actual ID (`credential.userId`) should be passed instead.

When credential sharing is enabled, `refreshOAuthTokens` returns a native Fetch `Response` (no `.data` property) instead of an Axios response in packages/app-store/zoho-bigin/lib/CalendarService.ts. The subsequent `tokenInfo.data.error` access will throw a `TypeError` at runtime.

Missing `!!` coercion in packages/lib/constants.ts: `APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED` evaluates to the literal `CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY` string (a secret) instead of a boolean. If this constant is ever logged or serialized, the encryption key leaks.

`z.object()` strips unrecognized keys by default in packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/parseRefreshTokenResponse.ts. Since the schema only recognizes `access_token`, all other OAuth response fields (`refresh_token`, `expires_in`, `token_type`, etc.) are silently dropped. This means `refresh_token` is always `undefined` and replaced with the placeholder string `"refresh_token"`.

`[z.string().toString()]` is a computed property that evaluates to a fixed literal string (like `"ZodString"`) in packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/parseRefreshTokenResponse.ts, not a dynamic key matcher. Use `.passthrough()` on the object schema instead.

The raw `fetch` `Response` is returned without parsing in packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/refreshOAuthTokens.ts. Callers access properties like `.data`, `.expiresIn`, `.expiryDate` on the return value, which don't exist on a `Response` object. The response needs to be parsed with `await response.json()` before returning.

When `APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED` is true, `refreshOAuthTokens` returns a raw `fetch` `Response` object in packages/app-store/hubspot/lib/CalendarService.ts. Casting it to `HubspotToken` silently yields `undefined` for `.accessToken` and `.expiresIn`, resulting in `NaN` expiry dates persisted to the DB.

When `APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED` is true, `refreshOAuthTokens` returns a raw `fetch` Response in packages/app-store/zohocrm/lib/CalendarService.ts, but this code immediately accesses `zohoCrmTokenInfo.data.error`. This will throw a `TypeError` at runtime.

When credential sharing is enabled in packages/app-store/googlecalendar/lib/CalendarService.ts, `refreshOAuthTokens` returns a native `fetch` `Response` (no `.data` property), but the next line accesses `res?.data` expecting a GaxiosResponse shape. This will cause a TypeError.

`parseRefreshTokenResponse` returns a Zod `safeParse` result (`{ success, data }`) in packages/app-store/googlecalendar/lib/CalendarService.ts, not the parsed value directly. Storing this as `key` corrupts the credential in the database. Access `.data` to get the actual credential object.
Webhook secret comparison in apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts is vulnerable to timing attacks. Use `crypto.timingSafeEqual` for constant-time string comparison.

Calling `parseRefreshTokenResponse` when credential sync is enabled in packages/app-store/office365calendar/lib/CalendarService.ts replaces the real OAuth `refresh_token` with the literal placeholder string `"refresh_token"`, which is then saved to the DB.

The parser call in packages/app-store/zoomvideo/lib/VideoApiAdapter.ts can return token data that does not match `zoomRefreshedTokenSchema` in credential-sharing mode, but the code still persists `refresh_token`/`expires_in` as if they were guaranteed.
The slug is hardcoded in packages/app-store/zoho-bigin/api/add.ts instead of using the already-imported `appConfig.slug`, creating an inconsistency with `callback.ts` in the same directory.
The `!parsedToken.success` check in packages/app-store/zoomvideo/lib/VideoApiAdapter.ts is now dead code since `parseRefreshTokenResponse` already throws an error internally when parsing fails.
The check `if (!accessTokenParsed.success)` in packages/app-store/salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts is unreachable since `parseRefreshTokenResponse` throws an error internally when parsing fails.
`response.statusText` is unreliable in packages/app-store/salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts—HTTP/2 omits it and some Node.js fetch implementations return an empty string. Use `!response.ok` instead.
No error handling for the fetch response in packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/refreshOAuthTokens.ts. If `CALCOM_CREDENTIAL_SYNC_ENDPOINT` returns a non-2xx status, the error is silently swallowed.
Webhook handler in apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts does not validate HTTP method. Should restrict to POST to prevent accidental triggers from GET requests.

`refreshOAuthTokens` can return the credential-sync response (which doesn't include Lark's `code`/`msg` fields) in packages/app-store/larkcalendar/lib/CalendarService.ts. `handleLarkError` will treat that as an error and throw.

Neither `symmetricDecrypt` nor `JSON.parse` is wrapped in error handling in apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts. If the encrypted payload is corrupted, this will throw an unhandled exception.

The missing encryption key is silently replaced with a hardcoded empty string `""` in apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts. Throw an explicit error instead of falling back to a useless default for a cryptographic secret.

When credential syncing is enabled in packages/app-store/webex/lib/VideoApiAdapter.ts, refreshOAuthTokens returns a payload that contains only access_token/expiry_date, but this adapter still validates against the full Webex refresh schema.

refreshOAuthTokens can return the credential-sync response in packages/app-store/office365video/lib/VideoApiAdapter.ts, but this path still treats it like a Microsoft refresh response, causing the credential to become immediately expired.

Unnecessarily roundabout import path in packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/createOAuthAppCredential.ts. Since this file is already inside `_utils/oauth/`, importing `../oauth/decodeOAuthState` navigates up then back down. The original `./decodeOAuthState` was simpler.
`CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` is out of alphabetical order in turbo.json — it should appear before `CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY`.

mesa.text: Webhook handler should explicitly enforce POST-only requests to reduce attack surface by rejecting unintended HTTP methods
Webhook authentication relies solely on static header comparison without HMAC signature verification, timestamp validation, or replay protection - intercepted requests can be replayed indefinitely
Schema validation errors will throw and result in a 500 response instead of catching validation errors and returning 400 with safe error messages
Decryption and JSON parsing can throw on malformed input (wrong encryption key, corrupted ciphertext, or invalid JSON) without error handling
No validation of the decrypted keys structure before storing - could store malformed credential data
findFirst + update/create pattern is vulnerable to race conditions if multiple webhook deliveries arrive concurrently for the same user+app, potentially creating duplicate credentials
Delegated refresh request has no timeout - if external endpoint hangs, refresh will block indefinitely
Delegated refresh request doesn't include any signature or shared secret to prove caller identity
Delegated refresh has no fallback to refreshFunction() if endpoint returns non-2xx or is unreachable
Delegated refresh has no error handling for network errors which will propagate uncaught
Zod schema definition is invalid - Zod doesn't support computed property names like [z.string().toString()] in object schemas, making schema check ineffective
Injecting synthetic 'refresh_token' value when absent masks protocol violations and can lead to storing invalid token state - OAuth providers expect existing refresh_token to be reused
APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED only checks for webhook secret and encryption key but refresh delegation also requires CALCOM_CREDENTIAL_SYNC_ENDPOINT, creating ambiguous partial-enabled state
Salesforce integration is not using refreshOAuthTokens utility like other integrations, so it won't support credential sync delegation when enabled
parseRefreshTokenResponse is passed googleCredentials which has already been mutated with new tokens instead of the raw token response, meaning schema validation checks the wrong object

codeant.text: parseRefreshTokenResponse Zod schema uses invalid syntax [z.string().toString()]: z.number() which does not work for matching 'any property that has a number value' - the minimumTokenResponseSchema is not functioning as intended
Inconsistent return/usage of parseRefreshTokenResponse - some modules expect ZodSafeParseReturn with success flag while others treat it as returning the parsed object directly
refreshOAuthTokens returns mixed types (fetch Response vs Axios response) without normalization, causing issues for callers expecting consistent interface
In GoogleCalendarService, parseRefreshTokenResponse validates googleCredentials instead of the actual refreshed token response (token)
parseRefreshTokenResponse returns safeParse result instead of parsed data, causing mismatch when assigning to Prisma
Webhook handler header comparison doesn't handle string[] type - headers values can be string | string[] | undefined
Webhook handler header name lookup may fail if env var contains uppercase since Node lowercases all headers
symmetricDecrypt is called with empty string fallback if CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY is not set, which will fail
No validation for encryption key length/format (must be 32 bytes for AES256)
JSON.parse(symmetricDecrypt(...)) may throw but is not wrapped in try/catch
Webhook handler does not log or trace failures for troubleshooting
Prisma query uses appMetadata.slug for appId but only selected slug from app lookup, not id - may cause type mismatch if appId expects integer id
No unit/integration tests added for parseRefreshTokenResponse, refreshOAuthTokens, or webhook handler
No rate-limiting or abuse protection on webhook endpoint
Decrypted keys shape is not validated before inserting into credential.key in database
Salesforce code uses response.statusText !== 'OK' check instead of relying on status code
.env.example comment about openssl rand -base64 24 is unclear whether key should be raw bytes or base64 encoded

kodus.text: Multiple integrations (Google Calendar, Webex, Zoho Bigin) experience runtime errors due to inconsistent return types from refreshOAuthTokens utility - some callers expect axios-style response with .data property while others expect raw fetch Response object, causing TypeErrors
Error handling in token refresh operations is inconsistent - Hubspot swallows errors silently while Salesforce bypasses the centralized refresh utility entirely
Hardcoded configuration value in Zoho Bigin redirect URI instead of using dynamic appConfig.slug, breaking code reusability
Feature flags don't properly validate all required environment variables before enabling functionality
Webhook decrypts incoming credentials but stores decrypted plaintext directly in database instead of re-encrypting with CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY for at-rest storage - critical security vulnerability
minimumTokenResponseSchema contains conflicting index signature requiring all properties to be numbers, which conflicts with access_token being a string, causing parsing to always fail
Function injects static string 'refresh_token' if key is missing, corrupting credentials for integrations that don't preserve the old token

Fallback assigns literal string 'refresh_token' as value when field is absent, which gets persisted to database and causes permanent integration breakage on next refresh cycle with invalid_grant error
When credential-sharing branch is active, refreshOAuthTokens returns raw fetch Response object but callers access .data property which is undefined on Response objects, causing TypeError crashes

claude-code.text: In zoho-bigin/lib/CalendarService.ts, refreshOAuthTokens on line 93 is passed credentialId (credential.id, the database row ID) instead of credential.userId, causing incorrect user lookup when credential sync is enabled

In salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts, the getClient method refreshes the OAuth token and saves it to the database, but then creates the jsforce.Connection using the stale credentialKey.access_token and credentialKey.instance_url from the original credential instead of the freshly obtained accessTokenParsed.data values

minimumTokenResponseSchema uses [z.string().toString()] as computed property keys which evaluate to '[object Object]' instead of dynamic key matchers, causing the schema to strip all properties except access_token from parsed token data when APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED is true

cubic-dev.text: Async function deleteScheduledEmailReminder is called in unawaited .forEach() loops at every call site (handleCancelBooking.ts:485, handleNewBooking.ts:966, workflows.tsx:212). Because .forEach() doesn't await async callbacks, deletion failures are silently lost and the caller proceeds without knowing whether reminders were actually cancelled. Consider using Promise.all(reminders.map(...)) at the call sites.

The immediateDelete path cancels the email in SendGrid but returns without deleting or marking the workflowReminder DB record as cancelled. This leaves an orphaned row that the cron job won't pick up (it only processes cancelled: true). Add a DB delete or update after the SendGrid cancel request.

Async functions deleteScheduledEmailReminder and deleteScheduledSMSReminder are called inside .forEach() without await, making them fire-and-forget. The returned promises are silently discarded, so the try/catch block around this code will never catch async errors — rejections will surface as unhandled promise rejections instead. Use Promise.all with .map() so the deletions are properly awaited and errors are caught.

If twilio.cancelSMS() throws, the prisma.workflowReminder.delete() call is skipped, leaving a stale reminder record that could still trigger an SMS. Wrap each operation in its own try/catch, or move the DB delete into a finally block so the record is always cleaned up.

The try/catch wraps the entire loop, so one SendGrid failure aborts cancellation of all remaining reminders and also skips the Promise.all that deletes successfully-cancelled records from the DB. Move error handling inside the loop so each reminder is processed independently.
Filter cancellation candidates to scheduled email reminders with a valid referenceId before calling SendGrid cancel; otherwise invalid batch IDs can break cancellation.

Use Boolean @default(false) instead of Boolean? for the cancelled field. A nullable boolean creates three states (true/false/null) that complicate queries. The CRON job in scheduleEmailReminders.ts that schedules unscheduled reminders does not filter out cancelled: true records — so a cancelled reminder whose scheduledDate falls between 1–72 hours from now can still be picked up and sent.

The awaited async calls to deleteScheduledEmailReminder and deleteScheduledSMSReminder should be wrapped with error handling at the call site. Although both functions have internal try/catch, the returned promises are currently not awaited, so any unhandled rejection would crash the Node process. Once the promises are properly collected and awaited via Promise.all, wrap that Promise.all in a try/catch or use .catch() to gracefully handle failures.
Await the async reminder cancellations (or collect them in Promise.all) in the reschedule handler. Otherwise the reschedule handler can finish before the DB/API cancellations complete.
Unused import: Prisma is imported but no longer referenced anywhere in workflows.tsx file (the Prisma.BatchPayload usage was removed with the deleteReminderPromise array).

mesa.text: Error handling is too broad - catches errors for both SendGrid API call and database deletion, making it difficult to distinguish which operation failed. If SendGrid cancellation succeeds but database deletion fails, the reminder could remain in database leading to retry attempts

Sequential loop for cancelling reminders could become a performance bottleneck with many reminders - each SendGrid API call blocks the next iteration. Should use Promise.all() or p-limit for controlled concurrency

Default path marks reminder as cancelled without tracking when this occurred or how many times cancellation has been attempted. Missing metadata fields like cancelledAt, cancelAttempts, and lastCancelError for retry logic and operational visibility

SMS reminders are immediately deleted from database while email reminders use deferred cancellation (marking as cancelled for CRON processing) - architectural inconsistency means different failure modes and race conditions between channels

Making cancelled field nullable (Boolean?) creates tri-state logic (null/false/true) which complicates queries - WHERE cancelled = true won't match null values, potentially causing bugs. Should migrate to Boolean @default(false)

Using immediateDelete: true for emails during reschedule is inconsistent with cancellation flow used elsewhere (handleCancelBooking.ts uses deferred cancellation) - could hit SendGrid's 100 pending cancellation limit during reschedule operations
15-minute eventual consistency window creates race condition where reminders scheduled within 15 minutes of cancellation may still be sent before CRON job processes them
Missing send-time verification - actual reminder send path should check reminder.cancelled status immediately before sending as defense-in-depth measure
No retry mechanisms or observability for failed cancellations - missing error tracking, retry metadata, and backlog monitoring
