cubic-dev.text: Changing `getCalendar` to `async` breaks the synchronous caller `getCalendarCredentials` in `CalendarManager.ts`. It calls `getCalendar(credential)` inside a `.flatMap()` callback without awaiting, so the `calendar` field will be an unresolved `Promise` instead of the actual `Calendar` instance. This will silently break calendar operations at runtime. The caller needs to be converted to use `Promise.all` or an equivalent async pattern.

The `await getCalendar(...)` call (and subsequent async operations) inside this `forEach` are not caught by the surrounding try/catch. Because `forEach` doesn't await the async callbacks, any rejection from the awaited import or deletion will produce an unhandled promise rejection instead of being logged. Switching to `await Promise.all(...map(...))` will restore the try/catch's ability to handle these errors.

Using `async` callbacks inside `forEach` results in fire-and-forget promises — `forEach` does not await the returned promises. Calendar/video deletions will run concurrently with the rest of the function (e.g., sending emails), and any rejection will be an unhandled promise rejection rather than a caught error. Replace `forEach` with `Promise.all` + `map` so deletions are properly awaited before proceeding.

The `async` callback inside `.forEach()` is fire-and-forget — `forEach` discards the returned promises, so `await getCalendar(credential)`, `await calendar?.deleteEvent(...)`, and the subsequent `apiDeletes.push(deletedEvent)` all execute after the outer function has already moved past this block. Calendar events for recurring bookings will silently fail to be deleted. Convert this to a `for...of` loop.

`async` callbacks inside `forEach` are fire-and-forget — the returned promises are never awaited, so the `try/catch` block cannot catch rejections and the deletions race with the subsequent email send. Use `Promise.all` with `.map` (or a `for...of` loop) to properly await each operation.

Dynamic `import()` calls should be wrapped with error handling (e.g., `.catch()`) to gracefully handle module load failures. Currently, if any app module fails to load, the rejected promise will propagate as an unhandled error to any consumer that awaits it.

Wrap the awaited `getCalendar` call in a try/catch (or otherwise handle the rejection) so that a failure for one credential doesn't abort processing of the remaining references and the subsequent booking-status update. Without error handling, a single failed async import leaves the booking in an inconsistent state.

The `await getCalendar(credential)` call is not wrapped in a try/catch. Since `getCalendar` now performs a dynamic `await appStore[...]` import, a failed import will throw an unhandled exception that propagates to the caller.

All `import()` calls execute eagerly at module load time since they're direct expressions in the object literal—this doesn't achieve lazy loading. Every property is now a `Promise<module>` that fires immediately, loading all 28 modules in parallel as soon as this file is imported. Wrap them in thunks to defer loading until actually needed.

The awaited dynamic import should be wrapped in a try/catch so that a failed module load produces a meaningful error message (e.g., which `dirName` failed) instead of an opaque rejection propagating to the caller (handlePayment.ts).

The awaited dynamic import should be wrapped in a try/catch. If `dirName` is invalid or the module fails to load, the `await` will throw and bypass the existing falsy-check guard on the next line, propagating an unhandled error to callers (deletePayment.ts).

This awaited dynamic import (`await appStore[...]`) can reject if the `dirName` is invalid or the module fails to load, and there is no error handling around it. The existing `if (!(paymentApp && ...))` guard only handles a resolved-but-incomplete value, not a thrown error (bookings.tsx).

The awaited dynamic import `await appStore[...]` has no error handling. If the import fails (invalid `dirName`, module load error), it throws an unhandled exception that aborts the entire cancellation handler (handleCancelBooking.ts).

The awaited dynamic import should be wrapped in a try-catch so that a failed module load is handled gracefully (e.g., returning `null`) instead of throwing an unhandled rejection up to every caller (getCalendar.ts).

Missing trailing newline at end of file in changedAnchor_en.properties. This can cause issues with certain text-processing tools and produces noisy diffs when a new line is later appended.
Missing trailing newline at end of file in noHtml_de.properties. POSIX defines a line as a sequence ending in a newline; some tools may warn or behave unexpectedly with an unterminated final line.

Use a parameterized List type when deserializing recovery keys for better type safety - using raw List drops compile-time type safety and can hide casting issues, should use List<String> with TypeReference<List<String>>

Avoid hardcoding the recovery-codes credential provider ID - the provider ID is hardcoded as string but should use RecoveryAuthnCodesCredentialProviderFactory.PROVIDER_ID constant to stay in sync and avoid string-mismatch bugs
Fix minor typo/spacing in error message - double space in 'deserialize  credential' should be 'deserialize credential'
Config alias 'delayed-suthenticator-config' appears to be misspelled and should likely be 'delayed-authenticator-config'
RecoveryAuthnCodeInputLoginBean calls get() on Optional without checking presence, which could cause NoSuchElementException when no recovery codes credential exists
In BackwardsCompatibilityUserStorage, raw List and List.class are used when serializing and deserializing recovery codes instead of typed List<String> with TypeReference<List<String>>

mesa.text: Fallback logic treats updateCredential returning false uniformly, but this could represent either an unsupported operation or a transient storage failure, potentially leading to split-brain state where credentials exist in both stores
Should discriminate between capability limitations and transient failures by checking supportsCredentialType() first or catching specific exceptions to determine whether fallback is appropriate

Federated-first precedence strategy could cause issues if fallback has created local credentials while stale federated credentials still exist, returning stale federated credential and hiding newly generated local recovery codes
Missing documentation about federated-first precedence rule and its implications, or conflict resolution logic such as preferring the most recent credential by timestamp across both stores

The .get() call on the Optional could throw NoSuchElementException if no credential is found, should add explicit check with more informative error message or document the precondition that credentials must exist
Recovery code validation uses .anyMatch() for simple equality check but codes should be single-use (removed after successful validation) to prevent replay attacks
Concurrent recovery code updates lack locking or versioning protection, parallel setup attempts could result in overwritten credentials with no guarantee of consistency

codeant.text: Unsafe Optional.get() usage in RecoveryAuthnCodeInputLoginBean - credentialModelOpt.get() is used without checking isPresent(), which will throw NoSuchElementException and cause a 500 instead of a controlled error/redirect
JsonSerialization.readValue uses raw List.class instead of TypeReference<List<String>>, resulting in loss of generic type information and lack of type safety
CredentialHelper.createRecoveryCodesCredential wraps IOException in RuntimeException without logging the error with context before rethrowing
BackwardsCompatibilityUserStorage.getCredentials and isValid catch IOException but error messages lack username and exception stacktrace for debugging
RecoveryAuthnCodesUtils.getCredential uses Optional.or() method which was introduced in Java 9 - may not work if project must support Java 8
BackwardsCompatibilityUserStorage.getCredentials should use TypeReference<List<String>> for JsonSerialization.readValue to preserve generics
BackwardsCompatibilityUserStorage.getCredentials logs error on deserialization failure but continues - consider failing faster or including cause in the log

kodus.text: Data loss in credential creation for external user storage - the method serializes only the generatedCodes list into JSON, discarding other essential metadata like creation timestamp and user label contained within the credentialModel
Unsafe Optional.get() call in RecoveryAuthnCodeInputLoginBean will throw NoSuchElementException if a user does not have recovery code credentials configured

Incorrect credential model reconstruction in getCredentials method loses persistent ID - creates a new RecoveryAuthnCodesCredentialModel instance instead of returning the one from storage, causing downstream consumers that rely on credential ID to fail

Unsafe Optional.get() call without presence check on result of RecoveryAuthnCodesUtils.getCredential() in RecoveryAuthnCodeInputLoginBean - when no recovery codes credential exists, Optional.empty() is returned and unconditional .get() throws NoSuchElementException
NullPointerException in getCredentials when user is not found in local users map - getMyUser returns null when username has no entry, and myUser.recoveryCodes is dereferenced without null guard

claude-code.text: Recovery codes in BackwardsCompatibilityUserStorage.isValid() are never consumed after use, allowing infinite reuse of the same recovery code
Recovery codes validation in BackwardsCompatibilityUserStorage.isValid() accepts any matching code rather than only the next sequential one, violating the security contract

RecoveryAuthnCodesFormAuthenticator.isRecoveryAuthnCodeInputValid() calls removeStoredCredentialById() which targets local storage, not federated storage, so cleanup would fail for federated credentials

In getCredentials(), recovery codes credential is reconstructed via createFromValues() which does not set an ID on the model, so the original ID is lost making federated recovery codes undeletable via admin/account API
disableCredentialType() does not handle recovery codes credential type, preventing credential deletion for federated users
getDisableableCredentialTypesStream() does not handle recovery codes credential type, preventing credential disabling for federated users

kodus-v2.text: Dual-path credential creation creates security inconsistency: createRecoveryCodesCredential sends raw (unhashed) recovery codes to user storage via UserCredentialModel.challengeResponse, while the local credential provider fallback stores hashed codes. This means federated storage recovery codes are not hashed at rest while local storage hashes them.

Unsafe Optional.get() call without isPresent() guard in RecoveryAuthnCodeInputLoginBean.java line 19 will throw NoSuchElementException if no credential exists, while the same utility call in RecoveryAuthnCodesFormAuthenticator.java properly guards with isPresent() check

qodo-v2-2.text: BackwardsCompatibilityUserStorage.getCredentials() returns a RecoveryAuthnCodesCredentialModel created via createFromValues() without setting the stored credential id, so AccountCredentialResource will expose a null credential id and CredentialDeleteHelper cannot match/remove the federated credential by id

RecoveryAuthnCodeInputLoginBean unconditionally calls credentialModelOpt.get() and getNextRecoveryAuthnCode().get(), which throws NoSuchElementException when no recovery-code credential is returned (common for federated providers that don't expose credentials) or when all recovery codes are already used

qodo-v22.text: Federated credential parse crash: After storing recovery codes in user storage, UI/authentication code may retrieve a federated CredentialModel that is empty (provider didn't override getCredentials) or metadata-only (missing secretData/credentialData) and then call Optional.get() / createFromCredentialModel(), causing login/setup flows to fail with runtime exceptions

Stale local codes remain valid: When recovery codes are successfully created in user storage, existing locally stored recovery-code credentials are not removed, so old local recovery codes can remain valid alongside the new user-storage credential

User-storage codes not removed: When recovery codes are exhausted, RecoveryAuthnCodesFormAuthenticator calls removeStoredCredentialById(), but for federated users this removes from Keycloak's credential store (federated DB) rather than the external user storage, so the user-storage credential is not actually removed/disabled

Nullable provider dereference: CredentialHelper.createRecoveryCodesCredential unconditionally dereferences the RecoveryAuthnCodesCredentialProvider fetched by hard-coded id, so a missing/unsupported provider will cause a NullPointerException when falling back to local storage creation

qodo-extended.text: CredentialHelper.createRecoveryCodesCredential serializes raw generated recovery codes list to JSON and passes it to userStorage via updateCredential(), which diverges from Keycloak's hashed RecoveryAuthnCodesCredentialModel format and can result in plaintext recovery codes being persisted by user-storage providers

In RecoveryAuthnCodesFormAuthenticator.isRecoveryAuthnCodeInputValid(), the credential is retrieved via RecoveryAuthnCodesUtils.getCredential() which returns credentials from external user storage first, but when all codes are used, removeStoredCredentialById() is called which only operates on Keycloak's internal store—so for federated users the removal silently fails and the stale credential persists in the external store

In CredentialHelper.createRecoveryCodesCredential(), when updateCredential() succeeds (user storage handles creation), the method returns without deleting any pre-existing recovery code credential from Keycloak's local storage, unlike RecoveryAuthnCodesCredentialProvider.createCredential() which explicitly deletes existing local credentials first. This can leave stale duplicate recovery codes in local storage that become active again if the user storage provider is later removed or disabled

In BackwardsCompatibilityUserStorage.isValid(), when a recovery code is validated successfully, the used code is never removed from the stored list. The normal Keycloak flow (RecoveryAuthnCodesCredentialProvider.isValid) calls credentialModel.removeRecoveryAuthnCode() and updateStoredCredential() to consume the code, preventing reuse. This allows the same recovery code to be used unlimited times for authentication

In RecoveryAuthnCodeInputLoginBean constructor, credentialModelOpt.get() is called without checking if the Optional is present. If no recovery credential exists for the user in either federated or local storage, RecoveryAuthnCodesUtils.getCredential() returns Optional.empty() and this call throws NoSuchElementException, causing a 500 error during the login flow

In BackwardsCompatibilityUserStorage.getCredentials(), the stored credential data (a JSON list of raw codes) is deserialized and passed to RecoveryAuthnCodesCredentialModel.createFromValues(), which hashes each code again. Since updateCredential() stores the raw codes as-is, the model returned by getCredentials() contains double-hashed codes, making the allCodesUsed() check in RecoveryAuthnCodesFormAuthenticator unreliable

cubic-v2.text: `getMyUser(user)` can return `null` for an unknown user, causing a `NullPointerException` at `myUser.recoveryCodes`. Add a null check like the other credential methods do (e.g., `isConfiguredFor`).

`getCredential(...)` can return a user-storage credential, but the code below still removes and evaluates it as if it were a local stored credential. That breaks the "last code used" cleanup/prompt logic for federated users and can also inspect the wrong credential when both sources exist.
Persisting recovery codes through the generic user-storage path can make them reusable for federated users, because user-storage validators are not required to consume a code after successful use.

The reconstructed `RecoveryAuthnCodesCredentialModel` loses the stored credential's ID. `createFromValues` does not call `setId()`, so the returned model will have a `null` ID. Add `model.setId(myUser.recoveryCodes.getId())` after the `createFromValues` call to preserve the original credential ID.

qodo-extended-summary.text: BackwardsCompatibilityUserStorage.supportsCredentialType claims support for recovery-authn-codes but disableCredentialType does not handle that type, so disabling recovery codes for federated users will not actually remove/disable them
RecoveryAuthnCodesCredentialModel.createFromValues() never calls setId(), so federated credentials have null ID causing NPE or silent failure when removeStoredCredentialById is called

Recovery code validation in BackwardsCompatibilityUserStorage.isValid() only checks if code exists via anyMatch() but never removes/consumes the used code, allowing same recovery code to be reused indefinitely
In RecoveryAuthnCodeInputLoginBean, credentialModelOpt.get() is called without checking isPresent(), causing NoSuchElementException if no recovery credential exists for the user
When all recovery codes are exhausted for federated users, removeStoredCredentialById() only removes from local storage, not from the user storage provider
Recovery codes in BackwardsCompatibilityUserStorage.updateCredential() are stored as plaintext JSON array instead of being hashed with SHA-512 like the normal Keycloak flow

Inconsistent storage format vs validation: getCredentials() calls createFromValues() which re-hashes codes, but isValid() reads raw credentialData and compares directly against user input without hashing

bugbot.text: Both UpdateCompatibilityCheck and UpdateCompatibilityMetadata contain identical feature-checking boilerplate at the start of their run() methods (checking if ROLLING_UPDATES is enabled, calling printFeatureDisabled(), exiting with FEATURE_DISABLED, followed by printPreviewWarning() and validateConfig()). This shared logic could be extracted to a protected method in the base class AbstractUpdatesCommand to eliminate duplication.

gemini.text: The documentation statement 'Otherwise, the {project_name} Operator will fail' is misleading - when the 'rolling-updates' feature is not enabled, the operator falls back to default update behavior instead of failing. The text should be rephrased to clarify that enabling the feature is required to use configurable update strategies, not to prevent failure.

copilot.text: The change from exit code 4 to 3 for RECREATE_UPGRADE_EXIT_CODE may break existing scripts or automation that rely on exit code 4 for recreate scenarios - this should be documented as a breaking change in release notes if the update-compatibility feature was previously released without the FEATURE_DISABLED exit code

cubic-v2.text: The default-strategy test case returns before the new rolling-updates feature flag is enabled, so the `null` branch no longer covers the rolling-update path

`picocli.exit()` calls `System.exit(4)` for non-zero exit codes, which is a hard JVM termination path designed for build failures. For a 'feature disabled' condition, this bypasses graceful shutdown and makes the subsequent `return` unreachable. Consider throwing a `PropertyException` instead, consistent with how other error conditions in these commands are handled, or setting the exit code on the `CommandSpec` to let picocli handle it through its normal lifecycle
Documentation warning is broader than the implementation: the default `<not set>` strategy still works without `rolling-updates`, so saying the operator will fail is misleading

registerListener is gated by ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ (v1) but calls management(...) which can return V2 implementations when ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ_V2 is enabled - if both features are enabled, listener can call V2 methods like clients().setPermissionsEnabled(...) which throws UnsupportedOperationException, causing runtime failures on client/group/role removal events
Unused import org.keycloak.admin.client.Keycloak should be removed (not referenced in test class)
Unused import org.keycloak.authorization.model.Scope should be removed (not referenced in this class)
Static import AdminPermissionManagement.TOKEN_EXCHANGE and the logger field are unused in this class - remove them to avoid dead code and style/lint violations

baz.text: Event listener only executes when ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ is enabled, but V2-only deployments enable ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ_V2 and leave the legacy flag false, so role/client/group removals no longer disable permissions and stale configs remain; should gate on the V2 feature as well (or both) so cleanup still runs

resourceStore.findByName uses server.getId() as owner while AdminPermissionsSchema creates client resources owned by resourceServer.getClientId(), so per-client resource is never found and wrongly falls back to all-clients; should look up by server.getClientId() so permission actually resolves

hasPermission(scope) loads resourceStore.findByName with server.getId() but the all-clients resource is created with owner resourceServer.getClientId(), so every all-clients permission lookup returns null and canManage()/canView() fail even when that scoped permission exists; should query by server.getClientId() (or remove the owner) to align with resource creation

greptile.text: The getResourceName method in AdminPermissionsSchema.java doesn't handle the Clients resource type - when called for a client resource, it falls through to return resource.getDisplayName() instead of properly resolving client names, which could lead to incorrect display names in the admin UI

qodo-v2.text: ClientPermissionsV2#getClientsWithPermission returns resource names for resources of type 'Clients', but per-object resources are created without setting type. This can return 'Clients' (not a client UUID) and/or miss client resources, which breaks callers that treat results as client IDs

If ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ (v1) and ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ_V2 are enabled together, v1 endpoints/listeners still run but AdminPermissions.management() returns MgmtPermissionsV2, whose ClientPermissionsV2 throws UnsupportedOperationException for v1 management-permissions operations, causing 500s for /clients/{id}/management/permissions and removal-event cleanup

PermissionClientTest relies on default client scopes list having at least two entries and stable ordering via get(1), which can cause IndexOutOfBoundsException or removing an unexpected scope if defaults change

devin.text: NullPointerException when getResourceTypeResource returns null in hasPermission - the method can return null through multiple paths (supportsAuthorizationSchema returns false, resourceType is null, type not found in schema, or findByName returns null), but the result is used directly in findByResource without a null check, causing NPE instead of gracefully returning false

greptile-v4.text: AdminPermissionsSchema.SCHEMA.getResourceTypeResource(...) can return null and is immediately passed to findByResource(server, resource) without any null guard, which will throw a NullPointerException at runtime

The private getEvaluationContext(ClientModel, AccessToken) method is defined but never invoked anywhere in this class - it appears to be dead code that should be removed or wired into the permission-evaluation path

getResourceName method does not handle the Clients resource type - when called for a policy with resourceType = 'Clients', it falls through to resource.getDisplayName() which returns the raw UUID instead of a meaningful client identifier

Target realm is silently dropped for V2 evaluator in cross-realm scenarios - the V2 path passes adminsRealm instead of realm to the constructor, causing permission evaluation to run against the wrong realm for master-realm admin scenarios

mra-max.text: New Clients resource type and mapping added to AdminPermissionsSchema introduces potential backward-compatibility issues, incorrect resource/scopes wiring, and behavioral changes in authorization checks for client-related operations

Inconsistent per-client resource naming between AdminPermissionsSchema (uses internal client id) and ClientPermissions (uses 'client.resource.' + client.getId()) risks misalignment of resource lookup and permission evaluation

ClientPermissionsV2 marks many methods as not supported and throws UnsupportedOperationException, indicating incomplete feature with potential runtime errors if these methods are invoked by existing code
Possible NullPointerDereference in ClientPermissionsV2.hasPermission when both client-specific resource and global all-clients resource are missing, causing resource.getScopes() to be called on null

V2 admin permission evaluator is conditionally enabled behind ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ feature flag, which can lead to environment drift if flag is not consistently enabled across environments or tests
Unsynchronized lazy initialization of ClientPermissionsV2 in MgmtPermissionsV2.clients() could race in multi-threaded contexts, leading to inconsistent state or duplicated resources

Resource naming contract gap between production code's two different naming conventions for Client resources (internal id vs 'client.resource.' + id) risks flaky tests and mismatches in test expectations

Heavy test suite updates to reference new Clients resource type and V2 permission checks increases risk of gaps in test coverage and potential failures if test infrastructure doesn't align with V2 flows
Tests for All-Clients vs Per-Client flows may be flaky due to potential resource-name resolution issues where per-client lookup uses clientId and All-Clients lookup uses serverId as resource names
UserPermissionsV2 uses user ID as resource name for per-user resources, highlighting risk that if per-client resources follow a different naming scheme, V2 code path may diverge and cause regressions

mra-ultra.text: setPermissionsEnabled throws UnsupportedOperationException on client deletion with V2 enabled - ClientRemovedEvent listener in AdminPermissions.registerListener calls ClientPermissionsV2.setPermissionsEnabled which throws UnsupportedOperationException, causing unhandled crash during client deletion because the listener guard only checks for V1 feature flag

Doc comment on requireView() incorrectly says it returns a value when the method is void - Javadoc says 'Returns true if canView() returns true' but requireView() is declared void and throws ForbiddenException if check fails

canMapClientScopeRoles (MAP_ROLES_CLIENT_SCOPE scope) is completely untested - testMapRolesAndCompositesOnlyOneClient tests MAP_ROLES and MAP_ROLES_COMPOSITE scopes but never tests MAP_ROLES_CLIENT_SCOPE, leaving the role-to-client-scope mapping enforcement path unverified

mra-b.text: V1 uses resource type 'Client' with name prefix 'client.resource.', while V2 uses 'Clients' as both type and name, preventing V2 from finding V1-created resources
Inconsistent findByName overloads (with/without ownerId) can yield null even when resources exist, causing subtle lookup failures across permission evaluation paths

ClientPermissionsV2 overrides many methods (setPermissionsEnabled, exchangeToPermission, mapRolesPermission, etc.) with unconditional UnsupportedOperationException throws, yet production code (AdminPermissions.registerListener) and tests invoke these methods when V2 is active, causing runtime failures

mra-a.text: Feature-flag mismatch: registerListener uses ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ while evaluators/management use ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ_V2, causing the provider event listener to not be active when V2 is enabled but V1 is not

V1 ClientPermissions creates per-client resources named 'client.resource.<id>' with type 'Client', while V2 ClientPermissionsV2 looks up resources by client.getId() under type 'Clients', causing permission evaluations to silently miss

ClientPermissionsV2 throws UnsupportedOperationException for interface methods (e.g. setPermissionsEnabled, isPermissionsEnabled, mapRolesPermission) that callers may invoke at runtime, causing hard failures

Missing null-check in ClientPermissionsV2.hasPermission(ClientModel, scope) - AdminPermissionsSchema.getResourceTypeResource(...) can return null, and passing null resource into PolicyStore.findByResource(server, resource) will trigger NullPointerException

entelligence.text: getClientsWithPermission only evaluates per-client resources and ignores permissions granted on the 'all-clients' resource, causing admins with global client permission to see no results - need to check resource-type permission first or include resource-type resource in evaluation

getResourceTypeResource returns a resource that is not checked for null before being passed to findByResource(server, resource), which will trigger a NullPointerException - need to add null check and return false if resource is not found

resolveClient function duplicates existing code from resolveUser in AdminPermissionsSchema.java (92% similarity) - should extract a generic helper method or use a shared BiFunction to decouple lookups

hasGrantedPermission function duplicates existing hasPermission code from ClientPermissions.java (95% similarity) - should move hasPermission to base class and have ClientPermissionsV2 call inherited method instead

createUserPolicy(ManagedRealm, ManagedClient, String, String, Logic) duplicates existing createUserPolicy code from UserResourceTypeEvaluationTest.java (90% similarity) - should remove local method and call static method in AbstractPermissionTest

sourcery.text: Javadoc for requireView() incorrectly describes a boolean return value instead of the thrown exception behavior - should document that it throws ForbiddenException when canView() is false

Permissions cleanup on removal events (RoleRemovedEvent, ClientRemovedEvent, GroupRemovedEvent) now only runs when ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ is enabled, which can leave stale permissions if the feature is disabled after being used or if resources are removed while it's off
Missing tests to cover MAP_ROLES_CLIENT_SCOPE for clients - PermissionClientTest only covers MAP_ROLES and MAP_ROLES_COMPOSITE but not the new MAP_ROLES_CLIENT_SCOPE scope
Missing REST-level tests to cover the new CLIENTS resource type and its identifier resolution (both client ID and clientId alias)
Several Javadoc comments in ClientPermissionEvaluator (e.g., requireView(), requireViewClientScopes()) describe the methods as returning a boolean even though they are void

In ClientPermissionsV2, getEvaluationContext(...) is private and unused, and related imports/constants (e.g., AccessToken, EvaluationContext, ClientModelIdentity, TOKEN_EXCHANGE) appear unused as well

mesa.text: Event listener checks Profile.Feature.ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ while evaluator selection logic elsewhere in the class uses ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ_V2, causing inconsistency that could lead to unexpected behavior when only V2 is enabled or during migration

codeant.text: Unused imports in AdminPermissionsSchema (ClientProvider, ScopeStore) may cause compiler warnings

Evaluating the 'all-clients' resource for every client permission check may be performance heavy - consider caching or short-circuit optimization
Methods in ClientPermissionsV2 throw UnsupportedOperationException('Not supported in V2') without clear logging/error messages for debugging
Test cleanup lambdas that locate policies by name could fail if name collisions occur across tests
Ensure all implementors of ClientPermissionEvaluator interface are updated to match new method signatures and semantics
Missing unit tests for resolveClient logic in AdminPermissionsSchema to verify both client id and clientId lookups work
Missing test coverage for behavior when 'all-clients' resource exists but has no policies
Missing null-safety helper methods in ClientPermissionsV2 when calling admin store factories
The kc.client.id base attribute in ClientPermissionsV2.getEvaluationContext should be documented if other policies or conditions rely on it

kodus.text: Inefficient permission evaluation in getClientsWithPermission method causes O(N) complexity by iterating through all client resources and calling hasGrantedPermission for each, triggering full permission evaluation per client - will not scale for realms with many clients

Test cleanup logic for created policies uses findByName to retrieve policy ID for deletion, requiring two API calls instead of one and is fragile - policy ID should be extracted from Location header of creation response
findAll call uses null for pagination parameters while @AfterEach cleanup uses -1, -1 - using null will trigger default API pagination returning only subset of permissions, causing flaky tests

UnsupportedOperationException thrown during ClientRemovedEvent cleanup in V2 mode - ClientPermissionsV2.setPermissionsEnabled unconditionally throws exception, breaking cleanup flow when clients are deleted with ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ feature enabled

Dead computation: getEvaluationContext method builds ClientModelIdentity-based EvaluationContext with kc.client.id attribute but is never called - all authorization evaluations execute without client identity, causing policies relying on kc.client.id to evaluate against wrong context

Label 'area/dependencies' could not be found and needs to be created before Dependabot can use it

kodus-v2.text: UnsupportedOperationException thrown on client removal when ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ_V2 is enabled - Line 77 guards on ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ, but management() returns MgmtPermissionsV2 when ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ_V2 is enabled, and ClientPermissionsV2.setPermissionsEnabled() unconditionally throws UnsupportedOperationException, crashing the ClientRemovedEvent handler. Same applies to RoleRemovedEvent and GroupRemovedEvent

NullPointerException in hasPermission(ClientModel, String) when getResourceTypeResource returns null - Line 214 finds no resource for the specific client, falls through to line 217 for 'all-clients' resource lookup, and if that also returns null, line 219 passes null resource to findByResource(server, resource) causing NPE

Inconsistent admin-role fallback between canManage(ClientModel) and canMapRoles/canMapCompositeRoles/canMapClientScopeRoles - canManage checks root.hasOneAdminRole(AdminRoles.MANAGE_CLIENTS) before evaluating V2 permissions, but the three map-roles methods skip admin-role checks entirely, breaking the principle that manage is a superset of map-roles

qodo-v2-2.text: AdminPermissionsSchema.init() exits when the admin-permissions client already exists, so realms enabled before this PR will not get the newly-added Clients scopes/resources persisted in the admin-permissions ResourceServer, causing getOrCreateResource() to throw missing scopes and ClientPermissionsV2 to NPE when assuming the Clients all-resource exists for fallback evaluation

ClientPermissionsV2.getClientsWithPermission() iterates resources by type Clients and returns resource.getName(), which can include the schema-wide Clients resource and miss per-client resources because getOrCreateResource() never sets a Resource type, causing NullPointerException in AvailableRoleMappingResource when it dereferences realm.getClientById(cid) without null checks and producing wrong role-mapping results

qodo-v22.text: ClientPermissionsV2.getClientsWithPermission() returns Resource.name values for resources of type 'Clients', which includes the schema's all-clients resource named 'Clients' and excludes per-client resources because getOrCreateResource() never sets a type on those resources. When a caller treats returned values as client UUIDs, a granted 'all-clients' permission yields cid='Clients' and causes a NullPointerException

MgmtPermissionsV2 now returns ClientPermissionsV2, but ClientPermissionsV2 throws UnsupportedOperationException for permission-management methods like setPermissionsEnabled/isPermissionsEnabled/resource/getPermissions. If both ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ and ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ_V2 are enabled, V1-gated endpoints/listeners still call AdminPermissions.management(...).clients().setPermissionsEnabled/isPermissionsEnabled and will crash at runtime

qodo-extended.text: ClientPermissionsV2 queries resources using ownerId=server.getId(), but AdminPermissionsSchema creates resources owned by resourceServer.getClientId(); this prevents finding the intended resources and makes permission checks incorrectly deny access

The event listener gates all cleanup logic (role removal, client removal, group removal) behind ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ (V1 only), so when V2 is enabled, role and group removal events are silently ignored even though MgmtPermissionsV2 inherits working roles() and groups() from MgmtPermissions

In ClientPermissionsV2.hasPermission(ClientModel, String), when no per-client resource exists, getResourceTypeResource() is called but can return null, and the null value is then passed to findByResource(server, resource) which requires a non-null resource, causing a NullPointerException

getClientsWithPermission incorrectly includes the global 'Clients' resource name in the returned set of client IDs, causing NullPointerException when downstream components like AvailableRoleMappingResource attempt to look up this client via realm.getClientById(cid)

The getEvaluationContext method is defined but completely unused within ClientPermissionsV2 since the only method that previously utilized it (canExchangeTo) was overridden to throw an UnsupportedOperationException

cubic-v2.text: The listener skips permission cleanup when only ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ_V2 is enabled, causing stale fine-grained admin permission resources to remain when removing a role, client, or group for the V2 implementation
Client resources are added but getResourceName() has no CLIENTS case, so these resources resolve to a null label instead of a client name

qodo-extended-summary.text: ClientPermissionsV2 uses resourceServer.getId() as the ResourceStore ownerId when looking up resources, but resources are created/owned by resourceServer.getClientId(), so lookups fail and client/all-clients scope checks can incorrectly deny access

In ClientPermissionsV2.hasPermission(ClientModel, String), when no specific resource exists for the client, getResourceTypeResource() can return null which is passed directly to findByResource(server, resource), causing a NullPointerException

The event listener in AdminPermissions.registerListener() wraps all cleanup logic in Profile.isFeatureEnabled(Profile.Feature.ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ) (V1), but ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ_V2 does NOT depend on V1, so when only V2 is enabled, client deletion leaves orphaned authorization resources

In ClientPermissionsV2.hasPermission(ClientModel, String), the loop over evaluated permissions does not filter by permission.getResourceId().equals(resource.getId()), unlike UserPermissionsV2.hasPermission(), which could cause incorrect permission grants when falling back to all-clients resource
The private method getEvaluationContext(ClientModel, AccessToken) in ClientPermissionsV2 is defined but never called anywhere in the class, making it unreachable dead code
